# Gravel God Race Database — Full Context

> 328 gravel, MTB, and bikepacking races rated on 14 criteria.
> Produced by Gravel God (gravelgodcycling.com). Generated: 2026-02-25

## Scoring Methodology

Each race is scored on 14 dimensions (1-5 scale):
logistics, length, technicality, elevation, climate, altitude, adventure, prestige, race_quality, experience, community, field_depth, value, expenses.

Overall score = round((sum of 14 scores / 70) * 100).

Tier assignment:
- Tier 1 (Elite): score >= 80, OR prestige=5 + score>=75
- Tier 2 (Strong): score >= 60, OR prestige=5 + score<75 (capped at T2)
- Tier 3 (Solid): score >= 45
- Tier 4 (Developing): score < 45
- Prestige 4 promotes one tier (but not into T1)

## Tier 1 & Tier 2 Races (98 races)

### Tour Divide
Tier 1 | Score: 94/100 | 2745 mi | 200,000 ft | Banff, Alberta to Antelope Wells, New Mexico | June | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/tour-divide/

The longest mountain bike time trial on earth—2,745 miles from Banff to Mexico, completely self-supported, pure amateur ethos. A 2,745-mile expedition along the Continental Divide from Canada to Mexico. The route passes through 5 US states and 2 Canadian provinces, crossing the Divide 30+ times. Terrain ranges from glaciated Canadian Rockies to Wyoming's Red Desert to Colorado's 11,000+ foot passes to New Mexico's rocky Gila Wilderness. Surface is roughly 90% unpaved—mostly gravel and forest service roads with some singletrack and paved connectors. The character changes daily as you ride south through changing ecosystems, elevations, and climates. Signature challenge: The complete package—2,745 miles of self-supported suffering through every climate zone, every terrain type, and every mental state across 2-4 weeks on the Continental Divide. Tour Divide is bikepacking racing at its purest and most extreme. 2,745 miles from Banff to Mexico, completely self-supported, no entry fee, no prizes, no sponsors—just you, your bike, and the Continental Divide for 2-4 weeks. It's not really comparable to other races; it's an expedition that happens to be timed. The combination of distance, elevation, terrain diversity, climate extremes, and complete self-reliance creates something unique in endurance sports. With only ~716 finishers in history and a 47-60% DNF rate, completing Tour Divide joins a very exclusive club. Yes if you have serious ultra-endurance experience and can commit 2-4 weeks to the Continental Divide. Yes if you can handle complete self-sufficiency across remote terrain. Yes if you want the ultimate test. Skip if you haven't completed multi-day self-supported events. Skip if you need race organization, aid stations, or support crews. Skip if you cannot repair your own bike.

### Unbound Gravel 200
Tier 1 | Score: 93/100 | 200 mi | 11,000 ft | Emporia, Kansas | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/unbound-200/

The gravel race by which all others are measured. Relentless. Not one killer climb, but 200 miles of constant attrition. At 200 miles, this is a full-day effort. With 11K+ feet of climbing, the vertical gain is relentless. Signature challenge: The heat. The duration. The Flint Hills don't negotiate. Heat adaptation isn't optional—it's survival. The combination of 200 miles and extreme temperatures creates a unique endurance challenge. Unbound is gravel racing's Mount Everest—not because it's the hardest course, but because it's the race that defines the sport. Riders don't 'do' Unbound casually. They plan for it, obsess over heat protocols, and show up knowing that crossing the line in Emporia means something. The course itself isn't revolutionary—rolling terrain, gravel, heat. What makes it legendary is the field, the atmosphere, and the weight of the finish line. If you finish Unbound, you've earned it. If you're asking whether Unbound is 'worth it,' the answer is probably yes—but only if you're willing to commit to the training, the logistics, and the reality that this race will test every assumption you have about your durability. It's not fun. It's not easy. But it's unforgettable. Non-negotiables: Heat adaptation protocol built into weeks 6-10; Flint-specific cornering and line selection skills; 9-hour dress rehearsal in week 9

### Traka 360
Tier 1 | Score: 91/100 | 224 mi | 11,000 ft | Girona, Catalonia, Spain | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-traka/

Europe's Unbound—360km of technical Mediterranean gravel with Garmin Hill waiting to break you at km 300. Relentless climbing on Mediterranean gravel. 85% gravel with rough sections better suited to MTB. Flowing early terrain gives way to rougher mid-course chunks. Signature challenge: Garmin Hill—the steepest, slowest, most brutal climb of the day, hitting when you have 300+ km in your legs. If you're serious about gravel and have any connection to European cycling culture, Traka is a must. It's not just a race—it's a pilgrimage to the sport's spiritual home. Where else can you suffer on the same roads that Pogačar trains on? Yes, if you want Europe's premier gravel experience and can handle 12-18+ hours of racing. Traka delivers WorldTour-level legitimacy, stunning Catalan terrain, and a field from 74 countries. Just be ready for rough sections, Garmin Hill after 300km, and run 44-50mm tires.

### Colorado Trail Race
Tier 1 | Score: 90/100 | 530 mi | 72,500 ft | Durango to Denver, Colorado | August | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/colorado-trail-race/

The Colorado Trail doesn't care about your FTP when you're pushing your bike through talus fields at 13,000 feet. The CTR is 530 miles of high-altitude punishment disguised as a trail race. You'll spend most of your time above 10,000 feet, where 70% singletrack means constant technical riding interrupted by mandatory hike-a-bikes. This isn't gravel racing—it's Alpine survival with a bike occasionally underneath you. Signature challenge: The altitude never lets up—you'll average over 10,000 feet for 530 miles while navigating technical singletrack that forces constant hike-a-bikes. Most riders don't realize that CTR's difficulty isn't the climbing, it's maintaining any rhythm when you're pushing your bike every few miles at oxygen levels that make sea-level riders dizzy just standing still. This is for riders who've already proven they can suffer for days, not weekends. You need legitimate bikepacking experience, gear dialed for everything from desert heat to alpine storms, and the fitness to hike-a-bike for miles without breaking. If you haven't done multi-day self-supported racing or think 530 miles sounds doable because you can ride centuries, stay home.

### Trans Am Bike Race
Tier 1 | Score: 90/100 | 4233 mi | 175,000 ft | Astoria, Oregon to Yorktown, Virginia | June | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/trans-am-bike-race/

The longest self-supported bike race on earth—4,200+ miles from Pacific to Atlantic, crossing 10 states, no support crews, pure transcontinental adventure. A 4,200+ mile expedition from Pacific to Atlantic following the historic TransAmerica Bicycle Trail. The route passes through 10 states, crossing the Continental Divide in Colorado and the Appalachian Mountains in Virginia. Surface is roughly 90% paved—quiet county roads, bike paths, and low-traffic highways. The character evolves dramatically: Pacific Northwest forests, Rocky Mountain passes, endless Kansas plains, Ozark rollercoasters, Ohio River farmland, and steep Appalachian ridges. This is America condensed into one transcontinental ride. Signature challenge: The complete American experience—4,200 miles coast to coast through every landscape, every climate, every mental state across 2-5 weeks of self-supported suffering. Trans Am Bike Race is the longest self-supported bike race on earth—4,200+ miles from the Pacific to Atlantic, completely self-supported, across 10 states and every American landscape. While Tour Divide claims the crown for toughest mountain bike ultra, Trans Am is the road equivalent: pure expedition cycling at maximum distance. The mostly-paved route is more accessible technically but no less demanding physically—175,000 feet of climbing over 2-5 weeks will humble any cyclist. With the historic TransAmerica Trail as its backbone and 50 years of trail angel culture, this is America's great cycling pilgrimage. Yes if you have serious ultra-endurance experience and can commit 2-5 weeks to crossing America. Yes if you can handle complete self-sufficiency across 4,200 miles. Yes if you want the ultimate American cycling pilgrimage. Skip if you haven't completed multi-day self-supported events. Skip if 200+ mile days for weeks on end sounds impossible. Skip if Kansas wind and Appalachian rollercoasters would break your spirit.

### Transcontinental Race
Tier 1 | Score: 90/100 | 3100 mi | 210,000 ft | Across Europe (route changes annually) | July | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/transcontinental-race/

The flagship European ultra—4,000-5,000km self-supported across a continent. Mike Hall's legacy. The ultimate test. A continental crossing by bicycle. Start and finish points change each year—London to Istanbul, Geraardsbergen to Thessaloniki, Roubaix to Istanbul, and now Trondheim to Kalamata. Free-route format means riders plan their own path between mandatory control points. Alpine passes, Balkan heat, multiple countries, zero support. The TCR isn't about any single course—it's about the concept: cross Europe by bike, self-supported, as fast as you can. Signature challenge: 4,000-5,000km self-supported across a continent. Free-route navigation. 40,000-64,000m climbing. 0-40C temperature range. Clock never stops. The ultimate test of human endurance. The Transcontinental Race is ultra-distance cycling's most iconic European event. Founded by Mike Hall in 2013, it established the template for self-supported racing that defines the sport. 4,000-5,000km across a continent, free-route format, clock never stops, completely self-supported. TCR doesn't compare to other races—it's the standard against which all European ultra races are measured. Finishing is a lifetime achievement. Winning is legend. Yes if you have 2+ years ultra-distance experience, can ride 300-400km days consistently, handle extreme sleep deprivation, and want cycling's ultimate European challenge. Skip if you haven't completed multi-day unsupported rides, can't commit to 6+ months training, or expect it to be 'just a long race.'

### UCI Gravel World Championships
Tier 1 | Score: 90/100 | 112 mi | 8,202 ft | Rotating (2024: Heerlen, Netherlands) | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/uci-gravel-worlds/

The only gravel race where you can actually call yourself World Champion—if you can handle 180km of whatever terrain the UCI throws at you. 180 km elite, 140 km age group. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Varies by location. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 180 km elite + World Championship field + October weather. Rainbow jersey. UCI Gravel World Championships is World Championship—180 km elite, 140 km age group. Founded 2022. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Rotating locations (2022-2023 Italy, 2024 Netherlands). 1,000+ riders (elite + age group). October autumn conditions (10-20°C, 40% rain). UCI license required (€100-€150 / $110-$165). If you want World Championship rainbow jersey and fastest field in gravel, this is it. If you need domestic/local race, skip it. International destination, prestige 5. You need a UCI license, the fitness for nearly five hours of World Championship pace racing, and the mental fortitude to suffer in front of 1,000+ of the world's fastest gravel riders. The course changes annually, so you're gambling on terrain, but if you can't handle 2,500m of climbing and October weather in Europe, stay home. This isn't about participation—it's about proving you belong with the global elite.

### Unbound XL
Tier 1 | Score: 90/100 | 350 mi | 14,000 ft | Emporia, Kansas | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/unbound-xl/

The longest gravel race that matters, where the Flint Hills strip away everything but what's left of your soul. Unbound XL is a 350-mile commitment to Kansas limestone gravel that slices tires and punishes poor line choices across the rolling Flint Hills. The course feels like prairie purgatory — constant undulations that never let you settle into a rhythm, with long stretches between gas station salvation points. Sharp, chunky gravel demands constant vigilance while the relentless rollers chip away at your soul one hill at a time. Signature challenge: The 75-mile gap between meaningful resupply points turns nutrition into a high-stakes chess match. Most riders crack not from the hills or the gravel, but from running out of food or water in the Kansas void where gas stations close at 10 PM and your mistakes compound in the darkness. You need to finish a 200-miler first, preferably two. The 36-hour cutoff sounds generous until you're grinding limestone in your 16th hour with 150 miles still ahead. Self-supported means what it says—no crew, no checkpoints, just you and whatever gas stations stay open past midnight in rural Kansas.

### Badlands
Tier 1 | Score: 89/100 | 497 mi | 52,493 ft | Granada, Andalusia, Spain | August | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/badlands/

The world's hardest gravel race—800km through Spanish deserts and alpine passes, completely self-supported. An 800km expedition through Andalusia's extremes. Desert badlands that look like Mars, Europe's only true desert (Tabernas), and the 3,396m Pico Veleta climb. Self-supported means you carry what you need between remote villages. The combination of distance, elevation, heat, and self-reliance is unmatched in gravel racing. Signature challenge: The complete package—800km of desert heat, alpine altitude, and multi-day self-supported suffering through some of Europe's most extreme terrain. Badlands is gravel racing taken to its logical extreme. 800km, 16,000m climbing, 40°C+ deserts, 3,400m alpine passes, completely self-supported. It's not really comparable to other gravel races—it's an expedition that happens to be timed. The combination of distance, elevation, heat, and self-reliance creates something uniquely brutal. Finishing Badlands is a lifetime achievement that earns permanent respect in ultra-endurance circles. Yes if you have genuine ultra-endurance experience and want the ultimate test. Yes if you can handle extreme heat and self-support logistics. Skip if you haven't completed multi-day bikepacking events or can't adapt to 40°C+ conditions.

### Crusher in the Tushar
Tier 1 | Score: 89/100 | 69 mi | 10,000 ft | Beaver, Utah | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/crusher-in-the-tushar/

The climber's gravel race—where thin air separates pretenders from contenders. Relentless vertical. You climb for 35 miles, then descend for 35 miles. Simple math, brutal execution. Signature challenge: The altitude. 10,000+ feet average means your power is down 10-15%. Plan accordingly. Crusher is what happens when you take a mountain stage of the Tour and add gravel. It's not for everyone—flat-landers and non-climbers will suffer disproportionately. But if you love vertical and want to test yourself against thin air, this is your race. If you love climbing and want a race that rewards it, absolutely. If you're a diesel-engine flat-lander, consider something else. The altitude alone makes this a specialist's race.

### Leadville Trail 100 MTB
Tier 1 | Score: 89/100 | 100 mi | 11,900 ft | Leadville, Colorado | August | mtb
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/leadville-100/

The mountain bike endurance race that defines the category. Bucket list for a reason. Out-and-back with one massive climb (Columbine) and relentless terrain. The altitude is the course. Signature challenge: Columbine. The climb to 12,600 feet breaks people. If you can't hike-a-bike at altitude, you can't Leadville. Leadville isn't the hardest 100-mile race technically. It's not even the most scenic. But it's the most meaningful. The history, the community, the belt buckle—finishing Leadville changes how you see yourself as an endurance athlete. It's a rite of passage. Yes—if you're serious about endurance riding and willing to commit to proper altitude preparation. No—if you can't take time to acclimatize or haven't trained specifically for high-altitude suffering.

### Belgian Waffle Ride
Tier 1 | Score: 87/100 | 120 mi | 10,000 ft | Del Mar, California | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-california/

The race where bike handling skills outweigh FTP and 40% learn what DNF feels like. Belgian Waffle Ride delivers 10,000+ feet of climbing across 120-135 miles (2025's 106-mile course being an outlier) mixing 70% pavement with 30% 'unroad'—dirt, gravel, single-track, sand, water crossings, rock gardens. The route uses Belgian-themed sector names marking specific suffering zones. Signature challenge: Six-hour cyclocross race where tire choice matters more than FTP. That 23% grade at mile 130 doesn't negotiate. Belgian Waffle Ride earns its 'Hell of the North County' nickname through relentless variety rather than single massive climbs. The course delivers 10,000+ feet of climbing across 120-135 miles mixing 70% pavement with 30% 'unroad'—dirt, gravel, single-track, sand, water crossings, and rock gardens. Founder Michael Marckx designed the route to expose riders who rely on draft in group rides but lack comprehensive bike handling and endurance skills. The 40% DNF rate validates the course's effectiveness at separating technical riders from pure power athletes, maintaining BWR's identity as an 'un-road' race rather than a pure gravel endurance test. Yes, if you have legitimate technical skills from cyclocross or mountain biking, can handle 12+ hour moving time, and want to test yourself against a course designed to expose weaknesses. The race rewards actual bike riding—handling rough descents, choosing correct lines through sand, managing energy across varied terrain. It's expensive and honest about your capabilities. Skip it if you're purely a power athlete with limited technical skills, have never ridden extended single-track or sand, or can't stomach California travel costs ($1,200-1,800 total). Also skip if you need grassroots intimacy—BWR offers professional production and competitive intensity, not community warmth.

### Torino Nice Rally
Tier 1 | Score: 87/100 | 435 mi | 52,493 ft | Turin, Italy to Nice, France | September | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/torino-nice-rally/

The quintessential European bikepacking pilgrimage - 700km crossing the Alps on legendary military roads for 25 EUR. A 700km pilgrimage across the Maritime Alps from Turin's Piazza Giambattista Bodoni to Nice's Rifugio Massacan. The route weaves between Italy and France across 10+ major cols, alternating between legendary Tour de France climbs and historic military gravel roads. 65% paved alpine roads, 35% gravel including the sublime Gardetta Pass 'Little Peru' and Via del Sale finale. Every day takes riders over passes above 2,000m. The sweet spot between 2,000-3,000m where peaks escape trees and sky becomes more blue. Signature challenge: The complete Alpine crossing - 700km, 16,000m+ climbing, legendary military roads, self-supported through the Maritime Alps from Italy to France. The Torino Nice Rally represents bikepacking at its purest. 700km crossing the Maritime Alps from Italy to France on legendary military roads, fully self-supported, for 25 EUR. It's not a race - it's a pilgrimage. The Gardetta Pass 'Little Peru' section is described as possibly the most mesmerizing gravel ride in the world. Via del Sale's finale is 30km of dream gravel. The refugio culture, the rolling community, the charity mission for Smart Shelter Foundation - this is what the bikepacking ethos looks like when executed with soul. Founded with Tour Divide inspiration, TNR has earned its legendary status. Yes if you want authentic bikepacking adventure without commercial racing pressure. Yes if you appreciate community, charity, and soul over competition. Yes if you're prepared for multi-day Alpine self-sufficiency. Skip if you need race infrastructure and support. Skip if rocky military roads will destroy your pure gravel setup.

### Big Sugar Gravel
Tier 1 | Score: 86/100 | 100 mi | 9,500 ft | Bentonville, Arkansas | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/big-sugar/

Fall gravel perfection—stunning scenery, world-class town, legitimately hard course. Relentless punchy climbing with technical descents. The Ozarks don't have long climbs—they have endless short ones. Signature challenge: The accumulated vertical. 9,500 feet sounds manageable until you realize it comes in 50+ individual climbs. Death by a thousand punches. Big Sugar takes everything great about fall gravel—colors, weather, community—and combines it with a legitimately hard course. The Ozarks' endless punchy terrain creates a unique challenge. Add Bentonville's cycling culture and you've got a complete experience. Yes if you love punchy terrain and want a complete cycling destination experience. Maybe not if you're strictly a flat-course diesel.

### Gravel Earth Series
Tier 1 | Score: 86/100 | 208 mi | 12,139 ft | Global (HQ: Girona, Spain) | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-earth/

Europe's premier gravel circuit—global adventure racing from Girona to Kenya to Iceland, built on passion over profit. A global circuit that delivers genuine adventure across continents. The Traka offers Mediterranean gravel with sea views and Catalan forest trails. Migration throws riders into Kenya's safari lands with wildlife encounters and Maasai villages. Iceland provides volcanic otherworldliness. The Oregon Trail delivers American multi-day stage racing. Each event is distinct, but all share the GES ethos: adventure over pure competition. Signature challenge: The sheer variety—committing to GES means racing Mediterranean forests, African safari lands, volcanic Iceland, and American mountain ranges. No other series delivers this breadth of adventure. Gravel Earth Series represents the soul of gravel racing—adventure, community, and global exploration rather than pure competition. Where Life Time offers production polish and prize money, GES offers riding with giraffes in Kenya, volcanic rifts in Iceland, and Mediterranean forests in Girona. It's 'more of an experience than a competition,' as founder Cristina Freixes puts it. The 2026 consolidation (from 23 to 12 events) and calendar controversies suggest growing pains, but the core experience remains world-class. Yes if you want adventure beyond typical gravel racing and value experience over prize money. Yes if you're European-based or seeking unique global destinations. Yes if community vibe matters more than production polish. Skip if you need significant prize purses, prefer USA-based racing, or can't handle the travel logistics and expenses.

### SBT GRVL
Tier 1 | Score: 86/100 | 108 mi | 8,189 ft | Steamboat Springs, Colorado | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/steamboat-gravel/

Champagne gravel at altitude with a three-day festival vibe that somehow doesn't ruin the racing. SBT GRVL serves up 108 miles of Colorado's famous 'champagne gravel' - smooth, fast surfaces that seduce you into thinking this will be easy. The 8,189 feet of climbing happens gradually across working ranch land, aspen groves, and sage-covered hills, with 70% gravel that ranges from hero dirt to loose chunky sections that'll humble your bike handling. Signature challenge: The thing nobody tells you about SBT GRVL: champagne gravel is fast until it isn't. The deceptively smooth surfaces let you burn matches early while the altitude and relentless rolling terrain compound every tactical mistake. By mile 80, that silky gravel feels like riding through wet cement, and you've got 28 miles to contemplate your pacing choices. If you can hold 20+ mph for five hours at 7,000 feet, the Black course rewards fitness with buttery gravel and legitimate competition. The 8 mph cutoff sounds generous until you're grinding into headwinds at altitude. Come undertrained and you'll get pulled to a shorter course, which isn't the worst fate given the scenery.

### Gravel Worlds
Tier 1 | Score: 84/100 | 150 mi | 10,000 ft | Lincoln, Nebraska (Sandhills Global Event Center) | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-worlds/

The OG grassroots gravel race—where the spirit of the sport lives on in Nebraska summer heat. Long, hot, and humbling. NOT flat—'hill after hill after hill' averaging 4-5% grade either up or down the entire 150 miles through iconic cornfields. 10K+ feet of climbing with no single killer climb, just relentless rolling. Mud possible in wet years. Signature challenge: The heat. August in Nebraska is oppressive. Heat adaptation isn't optional—it's survival. Gravel Worlds represents what the sport was before corporate money arrived. It's authentic, community-driven, and legitimately hard. The 'World Championship' name started as a joke but the race earned its reputation through genuine challenge. Yes if you value authenticity, community, and genuine challenge over polish. Yes if you can handle August Midwest heat. Not if you need corporate production value.

### Dirty Reiver
Tier 1 | Score: 83/100 | 130 mi | 14,100 ft | Kielder, Northumberland, England | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/dirty-reiver/

Britain's original gravel race where 4,300 meters of climbing meets technical terrain and British weather that doesn't negotiate. Classic British suffering. Cold, wet, hilly, and beautiful. Kielder Forest doesn't negotiate. Signature challenge: The combination: 4,300 meters of climbing on technical terrain in British weather. Any one factor is manageable. Together, they break riders. Dirty Reiver is what happens when you take American gravel ambition and drop it in British conditions. The result is a genuinely hard event that tests fitness, skills, and weather resilience. If you want to understand UK gravel culture, you do Dirty Reiver. Yes, if British gravel interests you. Dirty Reiver is non-negotiable for UK gravel credibility. Just be ready for the registration sprint, the weather roulette, and the technical terrain that separates prepared riders from hopeful ones.

### Gravel Locos
Tier 1 | Score: 83/100 | 157 mi | 7,884 ft | Hico, Texas | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-locos/

Free 150-mile Unbound tune-up on gorgeous Texas gravel where Texas heat is the great equalizer. 'The scenery around Hico was stunning, adorned with wildflowers, and the gravel paths were as smooth as other riders had mentioned.' Difficulty is about relentless rollers, heat, and length - not technical terrain. Signature challenge: The heat-soaked late race is the defining suffer zone. In normal years, cumulative rollers plus Texas heat near the end are what everyone talks about. In 2022, riders literally ran out of water. Gravel Locos is what happens when you make a 150-mile Unbound tune-up free. Pros use it as their final rehearsal. Amateurs get to ride with them on gorgeous Texas gravel for zero entry fee. The catch? Texas heat that can hit 120°F and aid stations that occasionally run dry. Yes if you want a legitimate ultra-gravel experience for free with international competition and legendary Texas hospitality. Gravel Locos proves you don't need a $250 entry fee to have a world-class gravel race. Just train for heat.

### The Mid South
Tier 1 | Score: 83/100 | 100 mi | 6,000 ft | Stillwater, Oklahoma | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/mid-south/

The early-season gravel race with the biggest heart and the most unpredictable conditions. Fast and tactical when dry. Unrideable mud slog when wet. The weather decides what race you're getting. Signature challenge: The weather lottery. You train for months but Oklahoma decides what race you're riding. The Mid South is what happens when someone builds a gravel race around unreasonable hospitality instead of unreasonable suffering. Bobby Wintle hugs every finisher. The DFL gets the biggest trophy. The community shows up like their lives depend on it. And the weather? The weather does whatever Oklahoma wants. When it's dry, you get a blazing-fast tactical race. When it's wet, you get an unrideable mud nightmare. Either way, you finish to live music, local beer, and a town that genuinely wants you there. This isn't the hardest gravel race. It's not the longest. But it might be the one with the biggest heart. If you value community, celebration, and authentic hospitality over predictable racing conditions—yes, absolutely. If you need to know what you're getting into before you commit—maybe reconsider. The Mid South is chaos wrapped in a Bobby hug. Some years it's fast and perfect. Some years it's a mud disaster. Some years it gets cancelled by wildfires. But the people who come back year after year aren't coming for the course—they're coming for the experience. Show up ready for anything. Leave with stories. Non-negotiables: Weather adaptation training for unpredictable conditions (cold, heat, wind); Red clay handling skills and mud preparation; Wind exposure training on exposed ridgelines

### Belgian Waffle Ride Cedar City
Tier 1 | Score: 81/100 | 127 mi | 6,500 ft | Cedar City, Utah | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-cedar-city/

Half the climbing of San Diego. All the suffering. Plus miles of playground sand and a soul-crushing mile-100 combo. '125 miles. 6k of climbing. 75% dirt. Starting at 5,600 ft. Technical riding and sand. Dry weather at elevation. Demoralizing.' The first 3.5 hours average 23 mph. The last 25 miles average pain. Signature challenge: The mile-100 sandy climb leading straight into rocky/washboarded descending is the signature 'soul-crusher' that repeatedly appears in every race report. BWR Cedar City is what happens when you take the Waffle formula and add playground sand, mile-100 suffer-climbs, and Utah weather roulette. Half the climbing of San Diego but arguably more suffering because of where the difficulty is concentrated. The first 3.5 hours at 23 mph feel sustainable. The last 25 miles feel eternal. Yes if you want a different BWR challenge that tests sand navigation, weather adaptability, and late-race mental fortitude. Cedar City punishes under-prepared riders more than they expect. Come with 40mm+ tires, cold-weather layers, and a pacing plan that saves matches for mile 100.

### Jeroboam
Tier 1 | Score: 81/100 | 186 mi | 23,000 ft | Erbusco, Lombardy, Italy | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/jeroboam/

Italy's gravel masquerading as a wine tour until 7,000 meters of climbing and mandatory night riding separate the romantics from the masochists. Jeroboam masquerades as gravel but delivers two-thirds asphalt—don't be fooled, this isn't easy road riding. The Lombard Prealps serve up Roman cobbles, vineyard doubletrack, and 30% mountain bike trails that'll have you hiking sections. Wine country never tasted this bitter. Signature challenge: The 4pm start guarantees you'll ride most of this beast in complete darkness, navigating technical terrain with a headlamp while your body shuts down. It's not the 7,000m of climbing that breaks people—it's doing it blind for 14 hours straight. Show up undertrained and the Lombard Prealps will humble you by kilometer 30. The 4pm start means you're grinding technical singletrack in the dark with dead legs, so your bike handling better be dialed. This isn't a gravel race—it's a 24-hour suffer fest through vineyards that thinks it's the Giro.

### Gran Fondo Strade Bianche
Tier 1 | Score: 81/100 | 86 mi | 6,560 ft | Siena, Tuscany, Italy | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/strade-bianche-gran-fondo/

The cycling bucket list—Tuscan white roads and Piazza del Campo finish like the WorldTour pros. A mini spring classic through Tuscan countryside. The strade bianche sectors are the stars—compacted clay roads that become dusty highways when dry and grey paste when wet. Between sectors, fast paved roads with constant punchy climbs. The finish on Via Santa Caterina into Piazza del Campo is cycling theater at its finest. Signature challenge: Via Santa Caterina—the 16% cobbled finish into Piazza del Campo where you either crush it or walk in front of thousands. Gran Fondo Strade Bianche is the ride every cycling fan dreams about—the same white roads, same sectors, same Piazza del Campo finish as the WorldTour race. It's not the longest or hardest gravel event, but it might be the most romantic. The Tuscan landscape, medieval villages, and Italian cycling culture create something unique. When you hit Via Santa Caterina's 16% finish and thousands cheer, you understand why cyclists make pilgrimages here. Yes if you love cycling culture and want the iconic experience. Yes if you can handle March weather variability. Skip if you want pure gravel racing—this is Italian road cycling with gravel sectors.

### Rebecca's Private Idaho
Tier 1 | Score: 80/100 | 103 mi | 8,500 ft | Ketchum, Idaho | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rebeccas-private-idaho/

The gravel pilgrimage that sells itself through pure experience. Big mountain beauty with sustained but manageable climbing. Not the steepest grades, but relentless. Signature challenge: The combination of distance, altitude, and relentless terrain. Nothing is crazy steep—but nothing is flat either. RPI is what happens when a legendary athlete shares her passion with the community. It's not the hardest race, not the longest, not the most technical—but it might be the most soulful. The combination of world-class scenery, genuine community, and Rebecca's personal involvement makes this special. Yes—if you can afford the trip and want to experience gravel at its most soulful. This isn't just a race; it's a community gathering hosted by royalty.

### Sea Otter Classic Gravel
Tier 1 | Score: 80/100 | 89 mi | 8,100 ft | Monterey, California | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/sea-otter-gravel/

A gravel race wrapped in North America's biggest bike festival. Experience > competition. Fast fire roads with coastal character. More about the experience than pure gravel suffering. Signature challenge: The distraction. There's so much happening at Sea Otter that the race can feel secondary. Stay focused or get caught up in expo madness. Sea Otter gravel is best understood as part of the larger festival experience. If you want a pure gravel race, look elsewhere. If you want to race gravel AND attend North America's biggest bike expo, this is your event. Yes if you want the full Sea Otter experience and gravel is your discipline. No if you're looking for a pure gravel bucket-list race.

### Unbound Gravel 100
Tier 1 | Score: 80/100 | 104 mi | 4,000 ft | Emporia, Kansas | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/unbound-100/

All the Unbound prestige, all the Kansas suffering, half the distance—still not easy. A 100-mile test through the Kansas Flint Hills. Leaves Emporia traveling north through Wabaunsee and Morris Counties. One checkpoint at Council Grove (~mile 50). Same brutal flint rock roads, rolling terrain, and open prairie as the 200—just compressed into a faster, more intense package. Signature challenge: The combination of tire-destroying flint, Kansas heat, and self-sufficiency with only one checkpoint. Simple but relentless. The 100 is designed as a launchpad to the 200—and it delivers. All the Flint Hills suffering, the Unbound atmosphere, the iconic finish, but in a (slightly) more digestible package. You'll still get humbled by Kansas. You'll still earn your finisher glass. And you'll probably start eyeing the 200 the moment you cross Commercial Street. Yes if you want the world-class Unbound experience in a (relatively) more accessible package. Yes if you're building toward the 200. Skip if lottery entry or Kansas weather are deal-breakers.

### Belgian Waffle Ride San Diego
Tier 1 | Score: 79/100 | 131 mi | 12,000 ft | Del Mar, California | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-san-diego/

Belgian suffering translated to sunny San Diego, where Double Peak turns roadies into believers and flatlanders into casualties. BWR San Diego masquerades as an American Belgian classic, trading cobblestones for bone-rattling dirt sectors and sandy washes across 23 off-road segments. You'll roll through 80 miles of pavement and 52 miles of 'unroad' — everything from rocky single-track at Lake Hodges to the infamous sandy traps of Bandy Canyon. The course saves its cruelest joke for last: Double Peak, a 4.5-mile climb that turns savage in the final mile when your legs are already cooked. Signature challenge: The Muur van Dubbelberg — Double Peak's final mile averages 11% with 20-23% pitches when you've already climbed 10,500 feet. The last 800 feet of elevation come in that brutal final kilometer, forcing riders to 'paperboy' diagonally across the road just to keep moving. Your FTP means nothing here. You need legitimate climbing legs and the patience to navigate technical single-track when you're already cooked. This isn't a pure gravel race—it's a Spring Classic with dirt sectors that'll shred tires and egos equally. Show up undertrained for Double Peak's 20% final pitches and you'll be walking in front of 2,800 witnesses.

### Little Sugar MTB
Tier 1 | Score: 77/100 | 30 mi | 3,500 ft | Bentonville, Arkansas | April | mtb
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/little-sugar-mtb/

The Ozarks' limestone will shred your tires and ego in equal measure across 60 miles of relentless technical punishment. 30 miles, 3,500 ft. 70% singletrack, 30% gravel/dirt. Bentonville MTB trails (Slaughter Pen). Technical Ozarks terrain. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: Technical Ozarks singletrack + punchy climbs. MTB skills required. Little Sugar MTB is new LTGP race (founded 2025). Pairs with Big Sugar Gravel (same weekend—MTB Saturday, gravel Sunday). 30 miles, 3,500 ft. 70% singletrack, 30% gravel/dirt. Bentonville MTB trails (Slaughter Pen)—technical Ozarks terrain. Life Time Grand Prix race (pro field: Keegan Swenson, Sofia Gomez Villafane). 1,000+ riders. Bentonville is MTB mecca (Walmart headquarters, world-class trails). Early April spring conditions (50-70°F). If you want LTGP MTB race in world-class trails, this is it. If you want gravel or dropbar, skip it (or do Big Sugar Sunday). MTB required. You need advanced MTB skills and bulletproof tires—this isn't gravel cosplaying as mountain biking, it's legitimate XC suffering on sugar-sharp limestone that sounds like riding on glass. The Ledges at mile 39 will humble anyone who hasn't spent serious time on technical singletrack. Skip it if you can't handle extended conga lines or if your idea of technical is a gravel road with potholes.

### Seven
Tier 1 | Score: 76/100 | 78 mi | 10,500 ft | Nannup, Western Australia | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/seven/

Australia's crown jewel—hosting 2026 Worlds with brutal climbs, wildlife hazards, and Western Australian wilderness. A relentless climbing race through Western Australian pine plantations and state forests. The 'parallel singletrack' roads are often just two tire tracks wide with grass in the middle. Sharp quartz grit and mica create constant flat tire risk. 12-13 categorized climbs with 3,200m total elevation in 125km—one of the highest climbing ratios in gravel racing. Signature challenge: The Crucible—the 20%+ grade climb on loose surface where 'I went to hell and back... gradients frequently touching 20%' is the common refrain. SEVEN is Australia's answer to the world's great gravel races—and in 2026, it will literally host the World Championships. The 125km course through Western Australian forests is brutally technical with 3,200m of climbing on sharp quartz gravel. The Crucible alone, with 20%+ grades on loose surface, separates this from smooth US gravel. It's a genuine adventure race in a location that surprises everyone who expected red desert. Yes if you want a bucket-list Australian adventure and handle technical terrain. Yes if you're chasing UCI Gravel World Series. Skip if the travel commitment to Western Australia is prohibitive.

### BWR Utah (Belgian Waffle Ride Utah)
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 84 mi | 9,843 ft | Cedar City, Utah | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-utah/

Cedar City's altitude strips away your excuses while loose rock strips away your skin—Utah's answer to European suffering with American scenery. 135 km, 3,000 m. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Utah red rock—high desert, technical sections. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 135 km + 3,000 m elevation + May heat (20-32°C). Utah red rock. BWR Utah is red rock adventure—135 km, 3,000 m elevation. Founded ~2021. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Utah red rock—high desert, technical sections. 500-800 riders. Cedar City, UT. May spring conditions (15-30°C, hot). BWR series entry ($180-$250). If you want Utah red rock scenery with technical climbs and waffles, this is it. If you need cool weather, skip it. May heat is the challenge. Show up if you can handle bike handling at 6,000 feet where thin air meets technical terrain. The 92% unroad isn't just marketing speak—it's sand, rock, and singletrack that punishes hesitant front wheels and rewards desert racing experience. Skip it if you think gravel means smooth fire roads or you've never raced above 4,000 feet.

### Migration Gravel Race
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 404 mi | 26,000 ft | Maasai Mara, Kenya | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/migration-gravel-race/

Four days through Kenya's Maasai Mara where heat, altitude, dust, and wildlife create the ultimate gravel adventure. Four days through the Maasai Mara ecosystem. Wildlife crossings. Cultural immersion. Volcanic terrain. It's a safari with a timing chip. Signature challenge: It's not one thing—it's everything. Heat. Altitude. Dust. Distance. Wildlife. Four days of sensory overload. Migration isn't a race you do for a time or placing. It's a race you do for the experience. Four days through Kenya's Maasai Mara with full support, wildlife encounters, and landscapes that rewire your brain. If bucket-list gravel exists, this is it. Yes, if you can afford it and can get a spot. Migration is the gravel adventure of a lifetime. No other race combines this level of support, experience, and pure adventure. Just be ready for the price tag and the cumulative stress of four consecutive days.

### The Rift
Tier 2 | Score: 79/100 | 124 mi | 6,400 ft | Hvolsvöllur, Southern Iceland | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-rift/

Iceland's volcanic gravel race where you literally cross between continents and experience weather that doesn't negotiate. Otherworldly. Black volcanic sand, lava fields, glacier views. It looks like Mars with better air. Signature challenge: The weather. Iceland doesn't care about your race plans. You can experience four seasons in four hours. The Rift is gravel racing as geological adventure. The course crosses the Mid-Atlantic Ridge—you literally ride between continents. The terrain is volcanic, the weather is unpredictable, and the landscapes are unlike anywhere else on Earth. This is cycling adventure at its most otherworldly. Yes, if bucket-list adventure matters more than racing. The Rift delivers geological pilgrimage, otherworldly terrain, and a story you'll tell forever. Just be ready for Iceland's weather and the expense of getting there.

### Belgian Waffle Ride Asheville
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 129 mi | 10,000 ft | Asheville, North Carolina | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-asheville/

BWR's Blue Ridge cousin serves up Belgian-style punishment with Southern humidity and 10,000 feet of Appalachian reality checks. 129 miles, 10,000 ft. Belgian-style: 60% gravel, 30% pavement, 10% dirt. Blue Ridge Mountains. Technical gravel sectors. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 129 miles + 10,000 ft + humid heat. Blue Ridge brutality. BWR Asheville is Blue Ridge brutality—129 miles, 10,000 ft elevation, Belgian-style mixed terrain (60% gravel, 30% pavement, 10% dirt). Founded 2018 as BWR series expansion. Technical gravel sectors (BWR signature—rough, chunky, steep). 800+ riders. Asheville tourist city. Late May/early June = 60-85°F + humid. 15-25% DNF. If you want Blue Ridge suffering and BWR challenge, this is it. If you want pure gravel or easy terrain, skip it. You need solid climbing legs and comfort on technical gravel—those steep, chunky sectors don't forgive weakness. The 129-mile distance isn't the killer; it's the relentless elevation combined with late May humidity that breaks people. Show up undertrained and you'll join the 25% DNF rate somewhere around mile 80 when the Blue Ridge climbs start compounding.

### BWR Montana (Belgian Waffle Ride Montana)
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 87 mi | 9,186 ft | Missoula, Montana | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-montana/

The Belgian Waffle Ride that actually lives up to its Belgian difficulty rating, where Montana's mountains separate the climbers from the pretenders. 140 km, 2,800 m. 75% gravel, 25% paved. Montana Rockies—mountain terrain, technical sections. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 140 km + 2,800 m elevation + Montana Rockies mountain terrain. BWR Montana is Montana Rockies adventure—140 km, 2,800 m elevation. Founded ~2022. 75% gravel, 25% paved. Montana Rockies—mountain terrain, technical sections. 500-800 riders. Missoula, MT (great town, good airport access). August summer conditions (15-30°C). BWR series entry ($180-$250). If you want Montana Rockies scenery with technical mountain gravel and waffles, this is it. If you need flat terrain, skip it. Mountain terrain is the challenge. Show up if you can sustain power at 7,000 feet and don't mind walking your bike through Hell's Kitchen when the grades hit double digits. This isn't about gravel handling—it's about whether your legs can push a big gear up endless climbs while your lungs remember what thin air feels like. Skip it if you think 9,000 feet of elevation sounds fun on paper.

### Grinduro
Tier 2 | Score: 77/100 | 60 mi | 7,800 ft | Multi-Location Global Series | — | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro/

Four timed segments scattered across 60 miles of Sierra Nevada dirt — race hard when it counts, cruise with friends when it doesn't. Grinduro flips gravel racing on its head - four brutal timed segments scattered across 60 miles of Sierra Nevada terrain, with social cruise sections between the suffering. You'll ride conversationally through spectacular mountain scenery, then absolutely torch yourself for 5-15 minutes when the clock starts ticking. Signature challenge: The format itself is the killer - you can't pace conservatively because only four segments count, but you can't go full gas because there are three more stages coming. It rewards complete riders who can climb, descend, and suffer repeatedly while managing effort across 60 miles of Sierra Nevada punishment. You need legitimate climbing legs for 7,800 feet and technical handling skills for those singletrack descents. The format saves you from pacing disasters but won't save you from being undertrained. Show up fit enough to go anaerobic for 10 minutes at a time across varied terrain, or you'll get shelled on every timed segment.

### Giro Sardegna Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 81 mi | 8,530 ft | Siniscola, Sardinia, Italy | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/giro-sardegna-gravel/

Mediterranean gravel with UCI points that actually feels like a vacation until the dirt sections remind you why your watts matter. 130 km, 2,600 m. 75% gravel, 25% paved. Sardinian mountains—rolling terrain, coastal views. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 130 km + 2,600 m elevation + May warmth. Sardinian mountains Mediterranean views. Giro Sardegna Gravel is Sardinian mountains—130 km, 2,600 m elevation. Founded ~2020. 75% gravel, 25% paved. Sardinian mountains—rolling terrain, Mediterranean coastal views. 500-700 riders. Siniscola, Sardinia (coastal town). May spring conditions (18-28°C). Affordable entry (€90-€130 / $100-$145). If you want Sardinian island gravel with Mediterranean views and affordable entry, this is it. If you need local/domestic, skip it. International destination race. If you can hold threshold power on loose surfaces and don't mind mixing beach starts with proper gravel circuits, this delivers. The 50km circuit format means your pacing mistakes get amplified—show up undertrained for those dirt sections and the second lap becomes survival mode. Solid choice if you want UCI World Series points without the full sufferfest of most qualifiers.

### Grinduro France
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 62 mi | 6,562 ft | Xonrupt-Longemer, Vosges, France | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-france/

Grinduro's inaugural European outing trades California dust for Vosges forests, keeping the party-to-pain ratio intact. Enduro format: 4 timed descent stages (technical singletrack), untimed climbs. 62 miles, 6,562 ft. Vosges mountains: steep, forested, technical. Rewards MTB descending, allows social climbing. Signature challenge: Technical singletrack. MTB skills required. Crashes possible. Grinduro France is the French edition of the international festival-race series. Same format as Germany (untimed climbs, 4 timed descents), same party atmosphere (live music, camping, expo), but French: wine instead of beer, Vosges instead of Eifel, French food instead of German. New venue in 2024 (moved to Vosges from Monts du Lyonnais) brings stunning mountain scenery. Most riders prioritize experience over results. Technical descents reward MTB skills but are optional—untimed climbs allow social riding. If you loved Grinduro Germany but prefer French wine and mountain views, this is it. If you can handle 100 kilometers of varied terrain without crying about gravel bike handling, this works. The 2,000 meters of climbing spread across French forest roads won't destroy you, but the mix of surfaces rewards riders who can adapt rather than specialists. Show up for the weekend festival vibe, not pure racing—Grinduro's DNA is equal parts suffering and beer.

### Grinduro Germany
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 62 mi | 6,562 ft | Hellenthal, Eifel, Germany | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-germany/

A weekend festival disguised as a gravel race where your descending skills matter more than your FTP. Unique enduro format: 4 timed descent stages (technical singletrack/doubletrack), all climbs untimed. Rewards MTB descending skills, allows social climbing. 62 miles, 6,562 ft elevation. Eifel volcanic landscape—German forest roads and technical descents. Signature challenge: First technical test. Sets the tone. MTB skills required. Loose, rooty, rocky. Grinduro Germany is what happens when you cross a gravel race with a music festival and MTB enduro. The format is unique: only 4 descent stages are timed (technical singletrack), while all climbs are untimed transport stages. You can climb at social pace, chat, stop for photos—then pin it on the timed descents if you want. Most riders are here for the festival: live music, beer garden, camping, expo. The racing is secondary. The Eifel region is stunning—volcanic landscape, German forest roads, near the Belgian border. If you want a chill weekend with friends, amazing scenery, and some optional racing—Grinduro delivers. If you want a serious gravel race, look elsewhere. If you can handle technical singletrack descents and care more about German beer than race times, this is perfect. The untimed climbs mean fitness matters less than bike handling—bring 42mm tires, a dropper post, and zero ego. Skip it if you want pure gravel racing or hate crowds partying while you're trying to suffer.

### Mammoth Tuff
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 100 mi | 7,500 ft | Mammoth Lakes, California | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/mammoth-tuff/

Altitude meets volcanic attitude in California's most honest sufferfest. 100 miles of High Sierra alpine gravel. 7,500 ft elevation, 7,880-8,000 ft altitude. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Smooth fast roads. Technical rating: 2/5. Altitude is the defining challenge. Signature challenge: Altitude at 8,000 ft. Smooth roads mean high speeds burn energy faster. Sea-level riders suffer. Mammoth Tuff is a UCI Gravel World Series qualifier at 8,000 feet. 100 miles of smooth High Sierra gravel. Altitude is the challenge—7,880-8,000 ft throughout means 30% less oxygen. If you're sea-level, arrive 3-5 days early or suffer. Course isn't technical (2/5), it's fast and smooth. Mammoth Lakes is expensive resort town. June Lake Loop (mile 40-60) is stunning. UCI status brings competitive field (Keegan Swenson, Lauren De Crescenzo). If you want High Sierra scenery with altitude suffering, this is it. Show up fit or the 8,000-foot starting elevation will expose every training shortcut you took. The 7,500 feet of climbing over 105 miles isn't negotiable, and volcanic gravel punishes both your legs and your equipment. This isn't a scenic tour—it's a legitimate training ground that's produced Unbound winners for a reason.

### Nordic Chase Gravel Edition
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 373 mi | 26,247 ft | Copenhagen, Denmark to Oslo, Norway | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/nordic-chase-gravel/

Three countries, 100 hours, and enough Scandinavian gravel to remind you why self-supported ultras don't negotiate. ~600 km, ~8,000 m. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Multi-day stage race (4 stages). Copenhagen to Oslo. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: Multi-day fatigue (4 days, 600 km). Supported but cumulative. Nordic Chase Gravel Edition is multi-day Scandinavian adventure—Copenhagen to Oslo, ~600 km over 4 stages. Founded ~2019. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Supported stage race (hotels, meals included in €400-€600 entry). 200-300 riders. 4 stages ~150km each. Scandinavian countryside—rolling terrain, coastal sections. June summer conditions (15-25°C). If you want multi-day European adventure with support, this is it. If you want single-day or budget race, skip it. Expensive but all-inclusive. You need serious self-supported experience and the mental fortitude for 100-hour efforts across three countries. The 60-80% gravel through Nordic forests isn't technical, but the distance and isolation will expose every weakness in your preparation and gear. Show up undertrained and you'll be arranging your own expensive extraction from rural Sweden.

### Oregon Trail Gravel Grinder
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 375 mi | 35,000 ft | Central Oregon (multiple towns) | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/oregon-trail-gravel/

The Grand Tour of American gravel—five days of Oregon's best, compressed into one transformative experience. Five distinct stages showcasing Oregon's diversity—high desert, forests, mountains, and everything between. Signature challenge: The cumulative nature. Any single stage is manageable. Five consecutive days of 60-80+ miles with major climbing is a different animal. Oregon Trail is for those who find single-day races limiting. The stage format creates different demands—recovery, consistency, pacing across days. It's closer to bikepacking than racing, with timing chips. Yes if you're ready for multi-day racing and want to experience gravel at its deepest. Not if you can't commit the time or prefer the intensity of one-day events.

### Whiskey Off-Road
Tier 2 | Score: 76/100 | 50 mi | 6,523 ft | Prescott, Arizona | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/whiskey-off-road/

The Southwest's 20-year MTB institution where Skull Valley tests your suffering and Whiskey Row rewards your survival. A greatest-hits tour of Prescott National Forest combining technical singletrack, flowing trails, exposed fire road grinding, and tactical racing. You start on historic Whiskey Row, climb through granite formations, suffer through Skull Valley, flow through Spence Basin, and navigate technical descents when your legs are cooked. Every type of MTB terrain represented. Signature challenge: Skull Valley—a 12-mile exposed fire road climb at the halfway mark. 2,500-2,700ft of gain when you're already depleted. Documented 102F readings in hot years. Where cramping becomes universal and mental fortitude separates finishers from DNFs. The Whiskey Off-Road is the Southwest's premier mountain bike festival—a 20-year institution that combines genuine physical challenge with exceptional event production. Skull Valley will humble you. The technical sections demand MTB skills. But the weekend package—historic Whiskey Row, live music, expo, community—elevates it beyond just a race. It's what happens when an event organizer spends two decades perfecting their craft. Yes if you want a world-class MTB festival experience with genuine physical challenge. Yes if you appreciate 20 years of event refinement. No if you're a gravel purist uncomfortable on technical terrain. No if you need budget-friendly entry.

### Chequamegon MTB
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 40 mi | 4,000 ft | Cable, Wisconsin (Chequamegon National Forest) | September | mtb
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/chequamegon-mtb/

Forty miles of Northwoods power suffering disguised as a mountain bike race that somehow became a gravel institution. 40 miles, 4,000 ft. 60% forest roads, 40% singletrack. Technical MTB terrain. Mass start chaos. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: Mass start chaos (3,000 riders) + OO section technical singletrack. Chequamegon MTB is legendary—40+ years (founded 1983), 3,000+ riders, mass start chaos. Life Time Grand Prix race (pro field: Keegan Swenson, Payson McElveen). 40 miles, 4,000 ft. 60% forest roads, 40% singletrack. Technical MTB terrain—roots, rocks, OO section legendary. Cable small Wisconsin town. Mid-September fall conditions (50-70°F). If you want MTB history, mass start chaos, and LTGP glory, this is it. If you want gravel or dropbar, skip it. MTB required. Show up fit or don't show up at all—the Seeley Fire Tower climb doesn't care about your weekend warrior training plan. You need sustained power for constant rollers and the mental fortitude to handle September weather that swings from dust storms to mud festivals. If you can hold 250+ watts for three hours while navigating lottery systems and tourist crowds, this delivers old-school racing credibility.

### UCI Gravel Suisse
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 68 mi | 11,300 ft | Aigle / Villars-sur-Ollon, Switzerland | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-fondo-switzerland/

The UCI's backyard playground where 3,300 meters of climbing separates tourists from gravel racers. Starting at cycling's global headquarters in Aigle, this course lulls you through 30 miles of Rhone Valley false confidence before unleashing 3,300 meters of vertical punishment. The second half abandons civilization for alpine forest tracks and 20% wooden ladders disguised as bike paths. It's less gravel race, more high-altitude survival exam with a fancy UCI stamp. Signature challenge: The Route des Chaux section after mile 49 strips away any illusion that this is a normal gravel race. Twenty percent pitches on technical forest tracks at 5,000+ feet elevation turn bikes into anchors and legs into concrete. The thing nobody tells you: it's not just steep, it's steep when you're already cooked from the altitude and previous 2,000 meters of climbing. You need legitimate climbing legs and a gravel bike that can handle 20% grades on loose surfaces. The second half destroys weekend warriors—if you can't sustain power on steep off-road climbs above 1,500 meters, the Route des Chaux will expose you. Come for the prestige and alpine scenery, but expect to earn every meter of that Col de la Croix finish.

### Gunni Grinder
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 120 mi | 10,000 ft | Gunnison, Colorado | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gunni-grinder/

High-altitude gravel that separates your actual fitness from your sea-level delusions. High-altitude mountain gravel with sustained climbs and big descents. 10,000 ft elevation gain over 120 miles at 7,700-10,000 ft base. Altitude is the defining challenge—oxygen is 25-30% thinner than sea level. Cochetopa Pass (miles 30-45) is a 15-mile sustained grind. High alpine sections (miles 50-70) max out altitude. Taylor Canyon rollers (miles 85-105) finish with relentless punchy climbs. This is a legitimate mountain challenge, not rolling gravel. Signature challenge: 15 miles of sustained climbing at 8,000+ ft elevation. Not steep, but relentless. Altitude makes every pedal stroke harder. Just keep spinning. Gunni Grinder is high-altitude mountain gravel done right. This isn't a flat power race—it's a climbing test at thin oxygen. You start at 7,700 ft (already higher than most people live) and max out near 10,000 ft. If you're from low elevation, your FTP will drop 10-15%. Your breathing will be labored. Your bonk will come faster. The Cochetopa Pass Road climb (miles 30-45) is a 15-mile grind that just keeps going—not steep, but relentless at altitude. The high alpine sections (miles 50-70) are exposed, windy, and stunning—above treeline with panoramic Rocky Mountain views. The Razor Creek descent is technical and loose—confidence-testing. Gunnison is a hidden gem: small town, big mountains, zero crowds. The post-race BBQ in the park is low-key awesome—burgers, local beer, live bluegrass. It's not a corporate expo. It's a community celebration. If you can sustain power above 8,000 feet and have low gears for Cochetopa Pass, this is honest mountain suffering without the Leadville circus. Sea-level riders thinking they'll adapt on race day will discover what oxygen debt feels like around mile 40. The terrain isn't technical, but the altitude will expose every fitness gap you've been hiding behind at home.

### Lauf True Grit Gravel Epic
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 84 mi | 9,000 ft | Santa Clara, Utah | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/lauf-true-grit/

Utah's technical playground where your handling skills matter more than your FTP and the Rollers of Doom separate pretenders from the genuinely prepared. True Grit drops you into the geological chaos where three deserts collide—Mojave, Great Basin, and Colorado Plateau. Eighty percent off-road through sandstone mazes, volcanic remnants, and sandy washes that chew up bikes and spit out undertrained riders. This isn't gravel racing, it's technical mountain biking disguised as a gravel event. Signature challenge: Lines through the rocks that riders practice a dozen times pre-race and still freeze up on race day. The technical sections aren't just difficult—they're bike-destroying, confidence-crushing puzzles that punish hesitation and reward local knowledge you don't have. Show up if you're comfortable dismounting on steep rock faces and practicing technical lines a dozen times before race day. The 9,000 feet of climbing rides like a century because of the constant technical demands—your bike handling needs to be sharp when you're deep into hour six. Skip this if you prefer smooth gravel roads or think a dropper post is overkill for gravel racing.

### Le Grand du Nord
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 110 mi | 5,000 ft | Grand Marais, Minnesota | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/le-grand-du-nord/

The most civilized way to suffer through 110 miles of Minnesota Northwoods while pretending Lake Superior views make your legs hurt less. Rolling terrain through Minnesota Northwoods. 5,000 ft over 110 miles = constant ups and downs, no sustained climbs. Mix of smooth gravel forest roads (70%), paved sections (20%), and technical doubletrack (10%). Smoother than mountain gravel but never flat. Signature challenge: Steep, scenic climb with Lake Superior views. Pack may still be together here—surges happen. Le Grand du Nord is Minnesota's entry into the UCI Gravel World Series—2,000+ riders racing through the stunning North Shore of Lake Superior at peak spring green. The course rolls through dense Northwoods, climbs for Lake Superior overlooks, and finishes in the tourist town of Grand Marais. It's not the hardest course (5,000 ft over 110 miles = rolling, not mountainous), but the field is international and fast. The scenery is world-class: dense Minnesota woods, Lake Superior vistas, and the iconic Gunflint Trail. Grand Marais is the perfect race town—hotels, restaurants, breweries, and post-race tourism. The large field means pack racing early and potential bottlenecks, but it thins out. This is a big-event, well-organized UCI race with all the amenities. You need the fitness for 110 miles and 5,000 feet—that's six hours minimum of rolling punishment through forest roads. The course isn't technically brutal, but it's relentlessly rolling, and those doubletrack sections at mile 80 will test your focus when you're cooked. If you can't handle pack racing with 2,000 riders or the possibility of 45-degree rain, stay home.

### Monaco Gravel Race
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 68 mi | 8,202 ft | Monaco | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/monaco-gravel-race/

A UCI qualifier that trades the usual gravel brutality for Alpine scenery and climbing legs that'll make you question your gear choices. 110 km, 2,500 m. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Monaco/French Alps—steep climbs, technical. Technical rating: 5/5. Signature challenge: 110 km + 2,500 m elevation = very steep. Monaco/French Alps. 20-25% DNF. Monaco Gravel Race is Monaco/French Alps—110 km, 2,500 m elevation. Founded ~2020. 70% gravel, 30% paved. Steep climbs, technical. 300-500 riders. Monaco start/finish (iconic location). October autumn conditions (12-22°C). Expensive entry (€150-€200 / $165-$220). If you want Monaco/French Alps iconic location with brutal steep climbs, this is it. If you need manageable elevation or budget race, skip it. 20-25% DNF. If you can handle 121 feet of climbing per mile on short, punchy ascents, this is your European gravel vacation with World Championship qualification upside. The Mercantour park setting means you're climbing through postcard scenery instead of Kansas cornfields. Skip it if you prefer sustained efforts over constant gear changes, or if you think 68 miles sounds easy because the math doesn't lie on those repeated kicks.

### Nova Eroica
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 84 mi | 9,186 ft | Buonconvento, Tuscany, Italy | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/nova-eroica/

Tuscany's gravel answer to L'Eroica that trades vintage wool for modern suffering on the same unforgiving strade bianche. 135 km, 2,800 m. 70% gravel (strade bianche), 30% paved. Tuscan rolling hills. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: 135 km + 2,800 m cumulative elevation. Tuscan strade bianche rolling hills. Nova Eroica is Tuscan strade bianche—135 km, 2,800 m elevation. Founded ~2015. 70% gravel (strade bianche), 30% paved. Rolling Tuscan hills, iconic white roads. 1,000-1,500 riders. Buonconvento medieval town. October autumn conditions (12-22°C). Affordable entry (€80-€120 / $85-$130). If you want Italian gravel with Tuscan scenery and strade bianche, this is it. If you want local/domestic race, skip it. International destination race. You need legitimate climbing legs for 9,186 feet of Tuscan punishment and the bike handling skills to descend white gravel roads that turn sketchy fast. This isn't a beginner's intro to European gravel—it's proper suffering in postcard scenery. Show up undertrained and those rolling hills will expose every fitness gap you've been ignoring.

### Ouray Gravel Grinder
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 80 mi | 7,800 ft | Ouray, Colorado | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/ouray-gravel-grinder/

The Ouray Gravel Grinder doesn't exist as an organized race, but the San Juan Mountains will humble your FTP and test your ability to push a bike up 13,000-foot passes. Ouray serves up 80 miles of high-altitude punishment across mining roads that were never meant for bicycles. You'll grind through San Juan valleys on County Road 23 before the real show begins—12,000-foot alpine passes where your 34x42 becomes a prayer. This isn't gravel racing, it's controlled mountaineering on two wheels. Signature challenge: Engineer and Cinnamon passes back-to-back above 12,000 feet, where even Tour de France climbers end up walking sections. The altitude turns your FTP into a cruel joke, and the loose rock surfaces make every pedal stroke a negotiation with gravity. You can't race what isn't there, but if you're drawn to self-supported suffering in Colorado's most brutal terrain, bring a mountain bike and your hiking legs. The Alpine Loop and Imogene Pass will have you walking more than riding above 12,000 feet, and afternoon thunderstorms don't negotiate with your Strava segment dreams. This is bikepacking country masquerading as a gravel destination.

### Pirinexus 360
Tier 2 | Score: 74/100 | 211 mi | 11,150 ft | Girona, Catalonia, Spain | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/pirinexus-360/

The thinking rider's 340k—half the distance of the big ultras but twice as beautiful, with a border-crossing climb that sends half the field home early. The Pirinexus is half smooth greenway cruise, half bike-destroying nightmare. You'll roll 55 kilometers on the silky Via Verde del Carrilet thinking this is easy, then spend the next 70 kilometers climbing volcanic hills and technical gravel that'll have you questioning your tire choice. The route crosses into France where the road literally disappears into rocky descents that punish anything under 40mm tires. Signature challenge: Col d'Ares is the course executioner—a 17.9-kilometer climb to the French border that peaks at 1,513 meters. It's not just the elevation; it's arriving there after 80 miles of volcanic punishment, knowing that technical descents await on the other side. Most riders call it quits here, and the ones who don't spend the descent walking their bikes down rocky trails that shouldn't exist on a cycling route. You need 200+ mile legs and 40mm+ tires minimum—the technical border descent after Col d'Ares will put narrower rubber on foot. This isn't a watts contest; it's navigation, bike handling, and knowing when to push through the Pyrenean climbs versus when to survive them. Show up undertrained and that 18-hour cutoff becomes very real very fast.

### Big Horn Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 85 mi | 10,000 ft | Gypsum, Colorado | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/big-horn-gravel/

Colorado's rowdiest gravel race where the 3,000 ft opening wall and rutted Jeep roads make pros switch to hardtails. 'A rowdy race with nearly 10,000 feet of climbing at elevation and a few miles of singletrack.' Rugged Jeep roads where 'you really need to keep your focus or you could crash pretty badly.' Signature challenge: The opening 3,000 ft in 13 miles is the signature gut-punch. Nearly every long-course discussion references that initial climb and how it sets up the rest of your day. Big Horn Gravel is what happens when you build a gravel race for people who think gravel races aren't hard enough. The 3,000 ft opening wall, the rutted Jeep roads, the technical singletrack - this is 'aggressive Colorado gravel' that makes pros switch to hardtails. Dylan Johnson got his biggest check here. The Vegan Cyclist calls it the best gravel race you've never heard of. It won't stay unknown for long. Yes if you want genuinely hard gravel racing that tests your technical skills, altitude tolerance, and pacing discipline. Big Horn is 'the best gravel race you've never heard of' - but it won't stay unknown for long. Come prepared for aggressive terrain and you'll have an unforgettable day.

### Grasshopper Adventure Series
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 70 mi | — | Northern California | January | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grasshopper-series/

The granddaddy of gravel serves up 70 miles of Northern California's hidden roads with enough climbing to remind you why it started this whole movement. The Grasshopper Adventure Series throws 70 miles of Northern California's most eclectic terrain at you—think wine country roads that suddenly dump you onto logging roads, then back to pavement without warning. This isn't just gravel grinding or road racing; it's bike handling boot camp where your line choice and gear selection get tested every few miles. The elevation gain sits somewhere between 4,500-9,116 feet depending on which Hopper you're riding, but the real challenge is the constant terrain switching that turns your legs into confused mush. Signature challenge: The Grasshopper doesn't just test your fitness—it tests your equipment choices and bike handling across terrain that changes every few miles. You'll climb 4,500+ feet while switching between pavement, packed gravel, loose dirt, and sometimes singletrack, making this less of a race and more of a northern California terrain survival course. Show up on the wrong bike or without solid handling skills, and you'll spend 70 miles getting schooled. If you can handle 4,500-9,116 feet of elevation on mixed terrain and navigate without hand-holding, this is your history lesson in what gravel racing actually is. Bring a gravel bike with 38-42c tires and the fitness for sustained climbing — the self-sufficiency requirement isn't posturing. Skip it if you need constant aid stations or can't fix a flat solo.

### Pisgah Monster Cross
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 50 mi | 8,000 ft | Brevard, North Carolina (Pisgah National Forest) | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/pisgah-monster-cross/

Pisgah's gravel roads will remind you that 8,777 feet of climbing doesn't negotiate, even when wrapped in Appalachian eye candy. 50 miles, 8,000 ft. 60% gravel, 40% singletrack/dirt. Pisgah National Forest. Technical Appalachian terrain. Technical rating: 5/5. Signature challenge: Technical Appalachian terrain (5/5) + steep (8,000 ft / 50mi). Pisgah Monster Cross is Southeast gravel classic—50 miles, 8,000 ft of technical Appalachian terrain. Founded ~2012. Pisgah National Forest. 60% gravel, 40% singletrack/dirt. Technical rating: 5/5—rocks, roots, steep descents. 300-400 riders. Brevard small mountain town. Late October fall conditions (40-65°F). If you want technical Appalachian gravel, this is it. If you want smooth roads or beginner-friendly, skip it. Dropbar MTB or gravel with MTB capability required. 15-20% DNF. You need legitimate climbing legs and comfort grinding for 14 hours through National Forest terrain that rewards bike handling over pure watts. The three aid stations mean you're mostly self-sufficient between long stretches of Pisgah's relentless rollers. Show up undertrained to this one and you'll discover new definitions of suffering before that 10PM cutoff.

### Rasputitsa
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 46 mi | 5,000 ft | Burke, Vermont | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rasputitsa/

Vermont's 10-year-old mud lottery where 5,000 feet of climbing meets April's bipolar weather and your cross training actually matters. 46 miles of Vermont Class 4 roads (unmaintained dirt). 5,000 ft elevation. Self-supported (NO aid). Mud guaranteed (April). Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: April mud + self-supported + Class 4 technical roads. Mud is guaranteed, aid is not. Rasputitsa is Vermont's early season mud fest—46 miles of Class 4 roads (unmaintained dirt), 5,000 ft elevation, self-supported (NO aid stations), and guaranteed mud. Founded 2016, 10th anniversary 2026. 'Rasputitsa' is Russian for 'mud season'. April in Vermont = mud. Class 4 roads = technical. Self-supported = hardcore. 600+ riders. Burke Northeast Kingdom. If you want Vermont hardcore suffering, this is it. If you want smooth gravel and aid stations, run away. If you can handle 46 miles of sustained climbing in conditions that range from perfect to apocalyptic, Rasputitsa delivers authentic Northeast Kingdom suffering without the ego. The 5,000 feet of elevation isn't negotiable, and April weather means you might get snow, mud, or sunshine—sometimes all three. Skip this if you need predictable conditions or hate climbing.

### The Rad Dirt Fest
Tier 2 | Score: 73/100 | 113 mi | 10,613 ft | Trinidad, Colorado | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-rad/

High-desert climbing race with Spanish Peaks views and 10,000+ feet of vertical. Long sustained climbs through high-desert Colorado. Spanish Peaks views and 10,000+ feet of vertical define the challenge. Signature challenge: The 18-mile climb to 9,000 ft. Long, steep, at altitude. This is where races are won and lost. The Rad is a climbing race set against stunning Spanish Peaks scenery. The 18-mile climb to 9,000 ft with grades up to 18% defines the event. Well-organized Life Time production with challenging terrain. Yes if you love climbing and want a challenging altitude race in stunning Colorado scenery. The 18-mile climb is the real deal.

### Belgian Waffle Ride North Carolina
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 131 mi | 13,000 ft | Hendersonville, North Carolina | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-north-carolina/

The Appalachian meat grinder that puts 29% fire roads and hike-a-bike sections in your face whether you're ready or not. BWR NC is the hell spawn of European classics and Appalachian brutality—40% unroad surfaces that mock your bike choice and 13,000 feet of climbing that laughs at your FTP. This isn't gravel racing, it's survival cycling through Blue Ridge punishment chambers where 29% fire roads meet creek crossings and your legs meet reality. The course dips into South Carolina just to add interstate humiliation to your suffering. Signature challenge: The hike-a-bike rate approaches 100% on certain sections—not because you can't ride them, but because the course design ensures traffic jams on unrideable singletrack climbs. BWR NC weaponizes bottlenecks, turning technical skill into patience training while your heart rate spikes from walking your bike up 25% grades. Show up if you've got a 30-tooth chainring, legitimate mountain bike handling skills, and the ego to walk your bike in front of strangers. The 13,000 feet of climbing through Carolina mountains will expose fitness gaps fast, and those singletrack bottlenecks mean pack position matters more than watts. Skip this if you think gravel racing should be mostly rideable.

### Gravel Brazil
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 62 mi | 6,562 ft | Balneário Camboriú, Santa Catarina, Brazil | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-brazil/

Brazil's first UCI gravel race arrives with World Championship stakes and zero track record—you're betting on potential over proof. 100 km, 2,000 m. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Brazilian coastal mountains. Steep climbs, technical descents. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: Steep Brazilian coastal mountains (2,000 m / 100 km) + technical descents + heat/humidity.
