Gravel Brazil is Brazilian coastal mountains—100 km, 2,000 m elevation. Founded ~2019. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Steep climbs, technical descents (loose gravel). 800-1,000 riders. Balneário Camboriú beach town. September spring conditions (18-28°C). Affordable entry (R$400-R$600 / $80-$120). If you want Brazilian gravel adventure with beach town vibes, this is it. If you want local/domestic race, skip it. International destination race. If you need UCI points or want to say you raced Brazil's inaugural gravel event, this makes sense. The 6,562 feet of climbing over 62 miles suggests sustained suffering, not punchy attacks. Show up ready for humidity, unknown surfaces, and the chaos that comes with first-year events where nothing goes according to plan.

### Heck of the North
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 100 mi | 4,500 ft | Two Harbors, Minnesota | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/heck-of-the-north/

Minnesota's sneakiest technical test disguised as a scenic fall colors ride. Constant rolling terrain with no extended flats. 4,500 ft over 100 miles = lots of short, punchy climbs. No sustained mountain-style climbs, but relentless ups and downs. 60% snowmobile trails (roots, rocks, narrow paths) make this a technical skills test as much as a fitness test. Think XC mountain biking on a gravel bike. Signature challenge: First extended technical test. Roots, rocks, narrow paths. If you lack MTB skills, this will be slow and scary. Sets the tone for the race. Heck of the North is what happens when you route a gravel race through Minnesota snowmobile trails at peak fall foliage. This is NOT smooth gravel—it's technical, rooty, rocky, narrow-path riding that rewards MTB skills, not just power. If you're coming from flat Midwest gravel races, this will surprise you. 60% of the course is on snowmobile trails: roots, rocks, tight turns, line-choice decisions. You're not just pedaling—you're bunnyhopping roots, picking lines over rocks, and keeping weight centered on loose descents. The fall foliage is stunning—peak color in late September means riding through tunnels of gold, orange, and red along Lake Superior's North Shore. The post-race vibe is classic small-town Minnesota: hot soup, sandwiches, and volunteers who genuinely care. It's a community center potluck, not a corporate expo. If you've got mountain bike skills and don't mind wrestling roots and rocks for 100 miles, this is your jam. The snowmobile trails demand 40mm+ tires and actual bike handling—your road racing background won't save you here. Skip it if you're looking for fast gravel or hate technical terrain that slows you to 8 mph.

### Rad Dirt Fest
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 62 mi | 8,202 ft | Salida, Colorado | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rad-dirt-fest/

The Rad serves up champagne gravel and Spanish Peaks views, but that 31-mile no-service stretch will expose every training shortcut you took. 100 km, 2,500 m. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Colorado Rockies—high altitude (2,200-3,000 m), technical climbs. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 100 km + 2,500 m elevation + high altitude (2,200-3,000 m). Colorado Rockies. Rad Dirt Fest is Colorado Rockies adventure—100 km, 2,500 m elevation. Founded ~2018. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. High altitude (2,200-3,000 m), technical climbs. 500-800 riders. Salida, CO (great town). September autumn conditions (10-25°C). Affordable entry ($150-$200). If you want Colorado Rockies high-altitude gravel with stunning scenery, this is it. If you need sea-level race, skip it. Altitude is the challenge. If you can hold steady watts for five hours at altitude without external validation, this is your race. The gravel is buttery smooth, but 8,900 feet doesn't negotiate with your sea-level fitness. Show up undertrained and that remote backcountry section becomes a very expensive therapy session.

### Southern Cross Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 100 mi | 5,000 ft | Dahlonega, Georgia | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/southern-cross-gravel/

North Georgia's wine country serves up proper climbing with a civilized finish—Woody Gap separates the tourists from the locals. Constant elevation change with no extended flats. Punchy, steep pitches (8-12% typical) spread throughout. This is NOT a power race—it's a climbing and bike-handling test. North Georgia Appalachian terrain: relentless rollers, technical descents, and forest road gravel. Signature challenge: The first major test: 1,200 ft over 5 miles (avg 5%, pitches to 12%). You're barely warmed up when it starts. Riders on road gearing walk here. Sets the tone for the day. Southern Cross is what happens when you build a gravel race for climbers, descenders, and wine drinkers. This is NOT Kansas. It's the North Georgia Appalachians—steep, punchy, unrelenting. The Montaluce Winery start/finish is chef's kiss: you suffer for 5-7 hours on red clay climbs, then roll into a vineyard where volunteers hand you wine, BBQ, and a chair with a mountain view. It's the only gravel race where you might actually want to stay at the finish line longer than it took to ride the race. The course is honest suffering—no gimmicks, just real North Georgia topography. If you're a diesel engine who thrives on sustained power, this will hurt. If you're a punchy climber who can recover on descents, you'll love it. If you can handle 5,000 feet of punchy Georgia climbs and aren't fazed by potential mud lottery conditions, this delivers solid suffering with the best post-race scene in the Southeast. Skip it if you need extensive aid station coddling or panic at the thought of 35-degree starts. Compact gearing and 40mm+ tires aren't suggestions—they're survival equipment.

### Vermont Overland
Tier 2 | Score: 71/100 | 54 mi | 7,000 ft | Woodstock, Vermont | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/vermont-overland/

Vermont's steepest 55 miles disguised as a pastoral New England postcard. 54 miles of Vermont pavé. 7,000 ft elevation. 60% gravel, 30% dirt, 10% pavement. 7 sectors rough unmaintained roads. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 7,000 ft elevation over 54 miles = relentless. Vermont pavé sectors = technical. Vermont Overland is OG gravel—founded 2009, one of oldest US events. 54 miles, 7,000 ft elevation. Vermont pavé—7 sectors of rough unmaintained roads like Paris-Roubaix but dirt. 500+ riders. Woodstock tourist town. Short distance (54mi) but brutal elevation (7,000 ft = relentless). Technical climbs + pavé sectors reward skills. If you want OG gravel history and Vermont technical terrain, this is it. If you want smooth roads, skip it. Those eight Class 4 sectors will humble anyone who shows up thinking this is a leaf-peeping tour. You need solid climbing legs for 7000 feet of punishment and bike handling skills for unmaintained roads that range from sketchy to bike-breaking. The 1000-rider field sells out in hours because riders who've done it know something the rest of us don't.

### Almanzo 100
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 100 mi | 6,500 ft | Spring Valley, Minnesota | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/almanzo-100/

The granddaddy of gravel racing where loose stone and 17% grades separate tourists from riders. The granddaddy of gravel racing rolls through Minnesota's Driftless region where glaciers never flattened the terrain. Six thousand feet of climbing over endless farm roads that transition from hard-pack early to soul-crushing loose stone as you fall apart. The hills never stop coming, and neither does the sensation you're riding through pioneer country on roads that were never meant for bikes. Signature challenge: Oriole Road at mile 90 is the soul-crusher that defines this race. GPS readings hit 17% grades on loose gravel when your legs have nothing left after 6,000 feet of climbing. It doesn't matter how strong you are—this hill breaks everyone. If you've got 6+ hours in your legs and can push through loose gravel that feels like riding in sand, this is gravel racing church. The Oriole Road climb at mile 90 will humble anyone undertrained, and the second-half stone layer turns strong groups into survival shuffles. Skip it if you need aid stations or can't handle Minnesota spring weather ranging from hypothermia-inducing rain to scorching sun.

### Bootlegger 100
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 100 mi | 10,000 ft | Dahlonega, Georgia (North Georgia mountains) | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bootlegger-100/

Wilson Creek Gorge will eat your lunch and serve it back with a side of North Carolina suffering. 100 miles, 10,000 ft. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. North Georgia mountains. Steep climbs, technical descents. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 10,000 ft / 100mi steep North Georgia mountains + technical descents. Bootlegger 100 is Southeast gravel staple—100 miles, 10,000 ft of North Georgia mountains. Founded ~2013. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Steep Appalachian climbs, technical descents (loose gravel). 500-600 riders. Dahlonega small mountain town. Mid-April spring conditions (55-75°F). If you want Southeast mountain gravel, this is it. If you want flat or beginner-friendly, skip it. Technical rating 4/5. 15-20% DNF. You need proper gravel legs and mechanical confidence before diving into Pisgah Productions' playground. The terrain doesn't negotiate, and being 100 miles deep in the NC mountains isn't the place to discover your limits. Show up undertrained and you'll spend more time walking than riding.

### Gravel Burn
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 62 mi | 8,202 ft | Mpumalanga, South Africa | October | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-burn/

A stage race trying to be Unbound with safari views, but the Karoo heat and seven-day commitment separate the curious from the committed. 100 km, 2,500 m. 85% gravel, 15% dirt. South African highveld—rolling red dirt roads. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: South African highveld rolling terrain + October heat. Red dirt roads. Gravel Burn is South African highveld classic—100 km, 2,500 m elevation. Founded ~2018. 85% gravel, 15% dirt. Red dirt roads (iconic South African). Rolling highveld terrain. 500-700 riders. Mpumalanga scenic region. October spring conditions (15-30°C). Affordable entry (R1,500-R2,000 / $80-$110). If you want African gravel adventure with stunning scenery, this is it. If you want local/domestic race, skip it. International destination race. If you've never done a multi-day gravel event, this isn't where you start—the Karoo semi-desert doesn't forgive poor pacing or inadequate hydration systems. You need solid base fitness for 100km+ daily efforts and the mental fortitude for a week of accumulating fatigue. The full-service camps cushion the suffering, but those long drags to places like Conical Peak will expose fitness gaps quickly.

### Grinduro California
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 60 mi | 6,500 ft | Quincy, California (Plumas County) | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-california/

A party disguised as a race where the descents are timed, the climbs are social, and your bike handling matters more than your FTP. ~60 miles, ~6,500 ft. Enduro format: 4-5 timed descents + untimed climbs. 50% singletrack, 30% gravel, 20% dirt. Technical rating: 4/5. MTB skills required. Signature challenge: Enduro format—timed descents reward MTB skills. Untimed climbs = social. Party vibe. Grinduro California is party gravel with timed segments. Founded 2015—OG location that spawned global Grinduro series (Germany, France, Scotland, Japan). Enduro format: 4-5 timed descents (compete) + untimed climbs (social). 50% singletrack, MTB skills required. 400-600 riders. Quincy small Sierra Nevada town. Legendary post-race party—beer, music, camping. Vibe: 60% party, 40% race. If you want serious racing, skip it. If you want fun event with technical terrain and epic party, this is bucket-list. Come if you want good vibes and can handle singletrack on drop bars—this isn't pure gravel racing. You need actual mountain bike skills for the technical descents and 40-45mm tires that grip. Skip it if you're chasing serious race results or can't ride technical terrain without white knuckling.

### Paris to Ancaster
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 62 mi | 4,000 ft | Paris to Ancaster, Ontario, Canada | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/paris-to-ancaster/

Canada's spring classic—where mud is the main competitor and surviving is winning. A multi-surface chaos ride through Ontario farmland. Fast flat rail trail sections contrast with hike-a-bike mud chutes. The racing is European spring classic style—surge for position before the bottleneck sectors, survive the mud, then sprint for the final climb. Point-to-point from Paris to Ancaster. Signature challenge: The mud chutes—deep clay sectors that turn racing into survival and break equipment and spirits. Paris to Ancaster is Canada's answer to Paris-Roubaix—a spring classic where mud is the defining variable. It's not the longest or hardest on paper, but the combination of cold, mud, and mechanical carnage makes it uniquely brutal. Founded in 1994, it predates the modern 'gravel' movement. If you want to test yourself in conditions that break bikes and spirits, P2A delivers. Yes if you want Canada's flagship gravel event and can handle mud chaos. Yes if you like spring classics vibes. Skip if you hate cold/wet or can't accept potential mechanical disaster.

### Rule of Three
Tier 2 | Score: 70/100 | 300 mi | 15,000 ft | Emporia, Kansas (same roads as Unbound Gravel) | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rule-of-three/

Three hundred miles on Unbound roads without the Unbound circus—just you, Kansas, and the kind of suffering that builds character. 300 miles, 15,000 ft. 95% gravel, 5% pavement. Kansas Flint Hills—same roads as Unbound. Ultra distance (15-25 hours). Technical rating: 2/5. Signature challenge: 300 miles ultra distance. Flint Hills rollers never stop. 15-25 hours. 30-40% DNF. Rule of Three is 300 miles on Unbound roads. Same Kansas Flint Hills rollers, but 3x the distance. Founded ~2018. Ultra distance (15-25 hours). Self-supported (aid stations but no SAG). ~100 riders. 30-40% DNF. Early September late summer heat (60-85°F). If you want ultra distance suffering on Unbound roads, this is it. If you want manageable distance or beginner-friendly, skip it. 300 miles is brutal. Mental game critical. This is for riders who want the full Unbound experience without paying Unbound prices or fighting Unbound crowds. You need legitimate endurance fitness—300 miles doesn't negotiate, and the self-supported nature means your mistakes compound quickly. Come prepared for mental warfare disguised as a bike race.

### Collegiate Gravel Nationals
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 62 mi | 4,921 ft | Turkey, Texas | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/collegiate-gravel-nationals/

The inaugural collegiate nationals that trades established prestige for first-year chaos in the Texas panhandle. 100 km, 1,500 m. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Texas Panhandle—rolling plains, red dirt. Technical rating: 2/5. Signature challenge: 100 km + Texas Panhandle wind + rolling plains. National Championship competitive field. Collegiate Gravel Nationals is USA Cycling National Championship—100 km, 1,500 m elevation. Founded ~2019. 80% gravel, 20% dirt. Texas Panhandle—rolling plains, red dirt. 200-400 collegiate riders. Turkey, TX (remote). April spring conditions (15-25°C, wind). Affordable entry ($50-$75). If you're collegiate and want National Championship, this is it. If you're not collegiate, you can't race. National Championship prestige. If you're college-aged with decent fitness and want to claim bragging rights as the first-ever winner, this is your shot. The 62-mile mix of gravel roads, rail trail, and singletrack won't destroy you, but March in Texas means unpredictable weather and zero race history to guide your prep. Show up ready for anything because nobody knows what they're doing yet.

### Highlands Gravel Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 67 mi | 4,850 ft | Goshen, Arkansas | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/highlands-gravel-classic/

The only US path to UCI Gravel Worlds—67 miles of Ozark smashfest. A concentrated smashfest through Ozark gravel. Tree canopy covers much of the course, providing shade but limiting descent visibility. The gravel is chunky and sharp—not smooth hardpack. Over a dozen climbs in 67 miles means you're either going up or bombing down. No recovery. Signature challenge: The relentless rollers on chunky sharp gravel—death by a thousand cuts with technical descents mixed in. Highlands Gravel Classic is the only US path to UCI Gravel World Championships—that alone makes it significant. The race itself is a 67-mile smashfest through chunky Ozark gravel with constant climbs and technical descents. It's short enough to race hard and long enough to hurt. For anyone chasing UCI qualification, this is mandatory. Yes if you're chasing UCI qualification. Yes if you want a short, intense race on technical terrain. Skip if you want ultra-endurance or smooth gravel.

### Rock Cobbler
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 100 mi | 5,000 ft | Bakersfield, California | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rock-cobbler/

The gravel scene's greatest shitshow — equal parts bike race, agricultural obstacle course, and fever dream cooked up by someone who thinks cyclocross isn't weird enough. 100 miles of Central Valley gravel and dirt. 5,000 ft elevation. 60% gravel, 30% dirt, 10% pavement. Chunky rocky sections. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: February weather roulette. Chunky rocks. Early season fitness test. Rock Cobbler is California's early season gravel classic—100 miles of Central Valley rocks, dirt, and Havilah climb. Founded by Stephen Fry in 2012. 600+ riders. February timing means weather roulette: 2023 was rain/mud (25%+ DNF), 2024 was perfect. Bakersfield is affordable ($80-120 lodging). Chunky rocky sections reward technical skills. Peter Stetina & Lauren De Crescenzo won 2024. If you're California-based and want early season test, it's great value ($80-$120). If you're flying in, weather risk is real. If you need your races predictable and your routes announced more than a week out, keep scrolling. This is for riders who want their fitness tested by 30% run-ups, cattle dodging, and whatever Sam Ames dreamed up the night before. Your FTP matters less than your ability to laugh while hike-a-biking through a ball pit. Show up undertrained and the scree fields will humble you in ways Strava can't measure.

### The Gralloch
Tier 2 | Score: 69/100 | 124 mi | 11,483 ft | Scottish Highlands, Scotland | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-gralloch/

Scotland's gravel crown jewel serves up 1,700 meters of climbing through Galloway's forests with a side of Highland hospitality. 200 km, 3,500 m. 75% gravel, 25% singletrack/dirt. Scottish Highlands—remote technical terrain. Technical rating: 4/5. Signature challenge: 200 km ultra + Scottish weather (40% rain, cold) + remote Highlands. 25-35% DNF. The Gralloch is Scottish Highlands ultra—200 km, 3,500 m elevation. Founded ~2017. 75% gravel, 25% singletrack/dirt. Remote Highlands terrain, technical sections. 200-300 riders. September autumn conditions (8-18°C, 40% rain). 25-35% DNF. Ultra distance (12-18 hours). Self-sufficient required. If you want Scottish ultra brutality with remote Highlands scenery, this is it. If you want manageable distance or good weather, skip it. Brutal but unique. You need solid climbing legs and comfort on technical gravel—that opening 11km ascent eliminates pretenders fast. The 4:15 PM cutoff at 67km isn't generous, so show up fit or get shuttled to the shortened route. Perfect for riders who want UCI World Series prestige without the chaos of American mega-events.

### BWR Arizona
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 104 mi | 6,600 ft | Cave Creek / McDowell Region, Arizona | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bwr-arizona/

Technical desert suffering through sand, rock, and singletrack—a true BWR experience in Sonoran landscapes. A desert gravel experience that emphasizes technical unroad over pure climbing. Sand washes, rocky singletrack, and punchy desert climbs through Sonoran landscapes. The BWR formula adapted to high-desert terrain. Signature challenge: The combination of sand, rock, and technical singletrack in desert conditions—'more unroad' means the terrain difficulty compensates for lower elevation gain. BWR Arizona delivers the BWR suffering formula in a desert package—technical singletrack, sand, rocks, and punchy climbs through Sonoran landscapes. It's less climby than California but compensates with unroad technical demands. Not an major series race, but attracts that caliber of competition. The 'lowest vert BWR' is still a beast. Yes if you want technical gravel racing with genuine unroad terrain. Yes if you're chasing BWR cred without major series pressure. Skip if you expect smooth gravel roads or hate sand and rocks.

### Sea Otter Fuego XL
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 70 mi | 7,800 ft | Monterey, California | April | mtb
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/fuego-mtb/

Sea Otter's sandbox climbs and corporate chaos create a perfectly mediocre gravel experience wrapped in mountain bike tradition. Fort Ord's sandy climbs and loose-over-hard descents turn your bike into a wrestling match. Two laps of relentless XC terrain where the sandstone gets looser and the ruts get deeper as 2,000 riders churn the course into a minefield of blown lines and shattered legs. Signature challenge: The sandy climbs don't negotiate with your fitness or technique. Fort Ord's sandstone terrain punishes every inefficient pedal stroke and rewards only those who've learned to dance with loose dirt. By lap two, the course has been churned into a minefield where clean lines disappear and mechanical sympathy becomes survival. If you can hold 250+ watts for three hours and enjoy fighting sandy ruts while dodging expo traffic, this works. The course isn't technical but those loose-over-hard descents will humble cocky roadies, and April weather swings from 40 to 90 degrees without warning. Skip it if you want pure gravel culture – this is MTB pageantry with drop bars allowed.

### Gravel and Tar Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 99 mi | 6,562 ft | Manawatū-Whanganui, New Zealand | February | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-and-tar-classic/

New Zealand's most beautiful way to discover that punctures and time cuts don't negotiate. 160 km, 2,000 m. 70% gravel, 30% paved. New Zealand countryside—rolling hills, farmland. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: 160 km long distance + 2,000 m elevation. New Zealand countryside. Gravel and Tar Classic is New Zealand countryside—160 km, 2,000 m elevation. Founded ~2018. 70% gravel, 30% paved. New Zealand countryside—rolling hills, farmland. 300-500 riders. Manawatū-Whanganui, NZ. February summer conditions (18-28°C). Affordable entry (NZ$150-$200 / $90-$120). If you want New Zealand countryside with long distance and affordable entry, this is it. If you need short distance or local/domestic, skip it. 160 km is long. International destination race. If you can handle 99 miles where the gravel sectors exist specifically to fracture the field and leave riders scattered across the Manawatū countryside, this is your kind of suffering. The 25% gravel ratio sounds friendly until you're dealing with punctures while chasing time cuts that historically claim a significant chunk of the field. Show up undertrained and you'll join the long list of DNFs who underestimated what 'toughest one-day race in Oceania' actually means.

### Gravel Roubaix
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 100 mi | 8,000 ft | Palmerton, Pennsylvania | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/gravel-roubaix/

A proper gravel beatdown disguised as a backyard Pennsylvania sufferfest. Barry-Roubaix throws everything at you except actual cobblestones. Eighty percent gravel roads with pavement breaks, sand traps, mud wallows, and one brutal mile of rocky two-track that separates pretenders from finishers. The terrain rolls constantly through Michigan farmland, never letting you settle into a rhythm. Signature challenge: Sager Road is the course's true character test—a narrow, unmaintained seasonal road that can be sandy, rocky, or muddy depending on conditions. It's where separation occurs in the front of the race because fitness alone won't save you when your bike handling skills get exposed on technical terrain at race pace. Show up ready for 8,000 feet of climbing and terrain that switches moods faster than spring weather. The Three Sisters will expose weak legs early, and Sager Road's sandy chaos separates pretenders from contenders. You need solid bike handling skills and the fitness to climb repeatedly—this isn't a gentle introduction to gravel racing.

### Grinduro Canada
Tier 2 | Score: 67/100 | 67 mi | 5,900 ft | Saint-Urbain-de-Charlevoix, Quebec, Canada | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/grinduro-canada/

A mountain bike race disguised as gravel, where meteorite-shaped terrain separates the prepared from the delusional. Grinduro Canada drops you inside a 450-million-year-old meteorite crater where Mother Nature has been plotting your demise since the Ordovician period. Four timed segments demand everything from singletrack handling to sand survival, connected by 'neutral' sections that aren't neutral at all. This isn't gravel racing—it's enduro with a gravel entry fee. Signature challenge: The course pretends to be gravel but demands full mountain bike skills across four timed segments that expose every weakness in your technical repertoire. Ancient meteorite impacts created terrain that laughs at road bike geometry and narrow tires—show up on the wrong equipment and the crater will humble you publicly. If you panic on technical singletrack or think 25mm tires are acceptable, stay home. This demands MTB skills, fat rubber, and comfort with getting your ego adjusted by Quebec sand pits. The festival vibes and excellent food soften the blow, but won't save you from walking the steep, loose climbs that eat narrow tires alive.

### Bend Dirt Fest
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 50 mi | 3,000 ft | Bend, Oregon | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/bend-dirt-fest/

A solid debut that prioritizes good vibes over gravel glory, perfect for testing Oregon's dirt without the Oregon trail suffering. High desert mix: gravel roads, doubletrack, singletrack. Multi-discipline festival course. 50 miles, ~3,000 ft. Oregon volcanic landscape. Signature challenge: High desert heat and sun exposure. Singletrack technical sections. Bend Dirt Fest is the Oregon Trail Cycling Series finale and a multi-discipline festival—gravel, MTB, trail run, all in one weekend. This isn't a pure gravel race; it's a celebration of Bend's outdoor culture. The course mixes high desert gravel roads, doubletrack, and singletrack. Field is small (~500 across all events), vibe is community-focused. If you're looking for a competitive gravel race, look elsewhere. If you want a fun weekend in Bend with friends, beer, and dirt, this is it. This works if you want a legitimate 50-mile dirt challenge without the circus atmosphere of bigger events. The 3,000 feet of climbing won't break anyone, but it'll remind you why base fitness matters. Skip it if you need competition depth or proven course logistics—first-year events always come with question marks.

### Lost River Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 85 mi | 8,500 ft | Mathias, West Virginia | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/lost-river-classic/

West Virginia's humid, hilly reminder that 8,500 feet of climbing doesn't need a single big mountain to break your legs. The Lost River Classic is Appalachian gravel distilled: constant elevation change through George Washington National Forest roads with no flat recovery sections. You're either grinding uphill on loose gravel or managing rocky descents that punish lapses in attention. The 85-mile course accumulates 8,500 feet via repeated moderate climbs rather than single dramatic ascents. Rocky, rutted descents with embedded rocks demand strong bike handling even when legs are cooked. Remote backcountry sections deliver genuine adventure feel with limited cell service and sparse support. This isn't a race with signature viewpoints or Instagrammable moments. It's 85 miles of honest work where heat management matters more than power output. Signature challenge: Managing 6-8 hours of constant elevation change and heat stress in August humidity with 30-mile self-supported sections. Not a single dramatic moment but sustained grinding endurance. The Lost River Classic doesn't pretend to be something it isn't. This is grassroots West Virginia gravel at its core: 85 miles of constant climbing through humid forest roads, rocky descents that punish mistakes, and August heat that compounds every effort. You won't find prize purses or Instagram influencers here. You'll find 250 regional riders who showed up to suffer together and support trail advocacy. The course delivers legitimate challenge—8,500 feet of elevation isn't a typo—but the real test is managing heat stress during 6-8 hours of constant work. Aid stations are 30 miles apart. Cell service is spotty. If something goes wrong, you problem-solve or walk out. That's the deal. If you want a well-produced national event with all the amenities, look elsewhere. If you want authentic Appalachian gravel racing where the challenge is real and the community actually cares about trail access, this is it. You need legitimate climbing fitness and wide-range gearing for the constant Appalachian rollers. August heat plus humidity makes this harder than the elevation suggests, and the rocky technical descents will punish weak bike handling. Show up undertrained and you'll be walking plenty of those punchy climbs in the remote backcountry sections.

### RADL GRVL
Tier 2 | Score: 66/100 | 81 mi | 8,202 ft | Adelaide Hills, South Australia | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/radl-grvl/

Australia's gravel monument that kicks off the international season with Adelaide Hills punishment and post-race shiraz. 130 km, 2,500 m. 85% gravel, 15% dirt. Adelaide Hills—rolling Australian bush. Technical rating: 3/5. Signature challenge: 130 km + 2,500 m cumulative elevation. Rolling Australian bush. RADL GRVL is Adelaide Hills adventure—130 km, 2,500 m elevation. Founded ~2020. 85% gravel, 15% dirt. Rolling Australian bush terrain. 400-600 riders. Adelaide city (good airport access). April autumn conditions (12-22°C). Affordable entry (A$150-A$200 / $100-$135). If you want Australian gravel adventure with good logistics, this is it. If you want local/domestic race, skip it. International destination race. If you can handle 2,300 meters of climbing on sketchy gravel roads while jetlagged, the Blue course rewards masochists with proper suffering through wine country. The Red gives you 60% of the pain for half the bragging rights. Skip it if you need perfect organization—this is only three years old and still figuring things out.

### Crystal Bear
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 192 mi | 8,000 ft | Laona, WI to Crystal Falls, MI | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/crystal-bear/

Crystal Bear is what happens when bikepacking meets gravel racing in the Northwoods—darkness, sandy sections, and 192 miles of self-reliant suffering. The Crystal Bear is an overnight point-to-point bikepacking odyssey through the Northwoods of Wisconsin and Michigan's Upper Peninsula. Unlike mass-start gravel races, this is self-supported adventure racing: you carry overnight gear, navigate independently, and resupply at small-town gas stations 40-60 miles apart. The terrain itself is moderate—rolling Northwoods gravel with 8,000 feet spread across 192 miles—but the overnight format is the real challenge. You'll spend 8-10 hours riding through pitch-black forest with only your headlight for company. Sandy sections sap momentum. Stream crossings require focus. Cold overnight temperatures (30-45°F) demand warmth management. Sleep deprivation compounds navigation challenges. This isn't about crushing segments or chasing KOMs. It's about holding it together mentally and physically for 15-30 hours straight while entirely self-reliant. Signature challenge: Managing 8-10 hours of solo darkness riding through remote Northwoods forest while fighting sleep deprivation, cold, and psychological isolation with zero external support. The Crystal Bear isn't about who's fastest. It's about who can hold it together when every external factor—darkness, cold, loneliness, sleep deprivation—is screaming to quit. This is overnight bikepacking racing at its purest: 192 miles of self-supported Northwoods gravel where you'll spend 8-10 hours riding solo through pitch-black forest with nothing but your headlight and your own thoughts. The terrain itself isn't brutal—8,000 feet over 192 miles is moderate climbing—but the format is the challenge. You'll fight sandy sections that kill momentum. You'll navigate stream crossings at 2 AM when your brain is mush. You'll stop to eat and start shivering within 5 minutes from overnight cold. And you'll do it all alone, with resupply points 40-60 miles apart and spotty cell service. This isn't a race for your first ultra-endurance event. It's for riders who've done the long stuff and are ready to test themselves against isolation and darkness. If you finish, you'll have earned something real. If you DNF, you'll learn exactly where your mental limits are. Either way, you won't forget it. You need overnight riding experience and the gear to match—this isn't your first gravel race with a bivy thrown in last minute. The sandy Wisconsin forest roads will humble roadie legs, and navigation errors in darkness can add hours. Come if you've done multi-day rides solo and understand that 'self-supported' means exactly that when you're flat-tired at 2 AM in the middle of nowhere.

### Dirty French
Tier 2 | Score: 64/100 | 100 mi | 9,000 ft | Chillicothe, Ohio | May | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/dirty-french/

Ohio delivers 9,000 feet of legitimate gravel suffering without the pretension of mountain racing. Dirty French borrows everything cruel from Ohio's rolling farm country and amplifies it through 9,000 feet of grinding climbs. This isn't manicured gravel—it's crushed limestone that turns to peanut butter when wet, farm roads that pitch up without warning, and long stretches where your GPS becomes your only friend. The Chillicothe region doesn't negotiate; it just keeps rolling upward until your legs forget what flat feels like. Signature challenge: Nine thousand feet of elevation over Ohio farm roads means you're climbing constantly, but never dramatically enough to justify walking. The gradient hovers in that cruel 6-8% range where you can't rest but can't power through either. By mile 70, every slight rise feels personal. You need solid fitness for 100 miles and 9,000 feet—this isn't a beginner's ride. The rolling Midwest terrain means constant power output with no real recovery sections. Come prepared for variable weather and mechanical self-sufficiency. If you can handle a 6-8 hour suffer-fest without mountain views as motivation, this will test you properly.

### Atlas Mountain Race
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 750 mi | 80,000 ft | Morocco | February | bikepacking
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/atlas-mountain-race/

Moroccan Atlas Mountains ultra-endurance gravel race. Atlas Mountains ultra-endurance self-supported gravel. Signature challenge: 750 miles and 80,000 feet through Atlas Mountains. Atlas Mountain Race is an ultra-endurance self-supported race through Morocco—750 miles with 80,000 feet of climbing. The Atlas Mountains are stunning and the adventure is extreme, but the very difficult access, extreme conditions, high costs, and self-supported format keep it in Tier 3. If you're an elite ultra-endurance rider with a big budget—maybe. For most riders—there are better options. Non-negotiables: Ultra-endurance training; Self-sufficiency skills; Budget planning

### Dirty 30
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 100 mi | 3,500 ft | Saranac, Michigan | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/dirty-30/

The Dirty 30 gets by on Michigan charm and distance options, but without signature terrain or serious prestige, it's more local weekend fun than destination race. Rolling Michigan gravel. Forest roads, rural gravel. 3,500 ft over 100 miles = constant rollers. No big climbs, just relentless ups and downs. Signature challenge: Constant rolling terrain—cumulative elevation without big climbs. April cold and mud risk. Dirty 30 is a Michigan Gravel Race Series event in April—meaning cold starts, mud potential, and early season fitness reality checks. 100 miles, 3,500 ft of constant rollers (no big climbs, just relentless ups and downs). Field is ~500, vibe is regional community. This isn't a destination race; it's a solid early season test for Midwest riders. If you're from Michigan or nearby, it's a good value ($45-$75). If you're flying in, there are better options. If you're in the Midwest looking for solid training miles with low pressure, this works. The 100-mile option gives you century bragging rights without the Unbound brutality, and starting from downtown Saranac beats another cornfield parking lot. Skip it if you need technical challenges or want to test yourself against serious competition—3500 feet over 100 miles won't break anyone who's been riding longer than six months.

### Iowa Wind and Rock
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 340 mi | 8,000 ft | Winterset, Iowa | April | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/iowa-wind-and-rock/

340 miles of Iowa gravel where the wind never stops and the suffering compounds every hour you're out there. Iowa Wind and Rock is 340 miles of honest Iowa gravel: crushed limestone roads rolling through endless agricultural land with zero shelter from wind. The terrain itself is moderate—8,000 feet of elevation spread thin via constant short climbs—but the format is brutal. You'll ride 30-50 hours continuously, crossing into overnight hours (often two nights for slower riders), navigating independently with GPS, and resupplying at small-town gas stations. The wind is THE defining feature: 10-20 mph sustained headwinds and crosswinds for hours. The constant rolling terrain means you never truly rest—always climbing or grinding into wind. River valley crossings add scenic variety and punchy elevation. The self-supported format strips away all external motivation. No crowds. No aid stations. No teammates. Just you, Iowa backroads, and the mental test of keeping pedals turning for 40+ hours when quitting would be easy. Signature challenge: Managing 30-50 hours of continuous riding while fighting relentless Iowa wind, sleep deprivation, and the psychological isolation of self-supported ultra-endurance racing with no external motivation. Iowa Wind and Rock is 340 miles of truth serum on gravel. You can't hide behind teammates, tactics, or short bursts of power. This is 30-50 hours of continuous forward motion where the wind grinds you down, the constant rolling terrain never lets you recover, and your own brain becomes the biggest obstacle. The terrain itself isn't hard—Iowa gravel is fast when dry, and there are no mountains. But the wind is relentless. You'll spend hours grinding into headwinds at 8-10 mph, watching your finish time balloon, questioning why you signed up. Sleep deprivation hits around Hour 25-30. Navigation errors happen. The loneliness of self-supported racing amplifies every doubt. And April weather is a lottery: rain turns the course into mud hell, cold overnight temps test your layering strategy, or you luck out with mild sunny conditions. If you finish, you'll have earned something rare: proof that you can keep going when every external factor and internal voice says stop. If you DNF, you'll learn exactly where your limits are. Either outcome teaches you something. You need 200+ mile experience and the mental fortitude to grind through 40+ hours of constant wind exposure across endless farm roads. This isn't about watts—it's about navigation skills, self-sufficiency, and accepting that you'll question every life choice around Mile 200. Skip it if you've never done overnight riding or think Iowa looks flat and easy.

### Ned Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 75 mi | 6,000 ft | Nederland, Colorado | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/ned-gravel/

High-altitude Front Range gravel with ghost towns and serious climbing. High-altitude mountain gravel with significant climbing through ghost towns. Signature challenge: High-altitude mountain climbing in Colorado Rockies through ghost towns. Ned Gravel is a high-altitude mountain gravel race in Colorado's Front Range with significant climbing and thin air. Nederland sits at 8,000+ feet, and the course climbs to 10,300 feet through ghost towns and mining country. The Colorado Rockies setting and altitude challenge elevate it above typical regional events, but the field remains mostly regional. Near Boulder with moderate access. Well-organized with four distance options. If you're in Colorado and want a mountain gravel challenge near Boulder with real altitude—yes, it's a solid event. If you're from out of state—there are more prestigious options. Non-negotiables: Altitude acclimatization; Mountain climbing endurance; Mountain weather preparation

### USA Cycling Gravel Nationals
Tier 2 | Score: 63/100 | 131 mi | 7,000 ft | La Crescent, Minnesota (2025-26); previously Gering, Nebraska (2023-24) | September | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/usa-cycling-gravel-nationals/

The national championship with open entry—stars and stripes on Driftless gravel with UCI Worlds pathway. Driftless Region 'champagne gravel' with punchy blufftop climbs. The terrain looks Midwestern-gentle but delivers relentless 10-15% gradients throughout. Apple orchards, gravel quarries, and river valley views provide scenery between efforts. Signature challenge: The relentless punchy climbs—not big sustained efforts, but repeated 10-15% gradients that accumulate suffering throughout the championship distance. USA Cycling Gravel Nationals delivers what the name promises—a legitimate national championship with open entry and UCI Worlds qualification. The Driftless Region course is harder than it looks with punchy 10-15% climbs and 'champagne gravel' that gets loose when freshly graded. Young event (est. 2023) finding its identity, but the jersey means something. Yes if you want a legitimate national championship jersey and Worlds qualification. Yes if you appreciate accessible championship racing. Skip if you want destination adventure over competitive credentialing.

Vapor Trail 125 is a 125-mile high-altitude gravel race in Leadville, Colorado—mountain terrain with extreme altitude (10,000ft+). The combination of 125 miles and extreme altitude is a serious challenge, but the moderate organization and regional field keep it in Tier 3. Leadville provides moderate access. Summer mountain weather at altitude adds complexity. If you want a high-altitude endurance test—yes, it's a serious challenge. If you want organization or pro field depth—look elsewhere. ### Cohutta Gravel Grinder

Cohutta Gravel Grinder is a 100-mile mountain gravel race through north Georgia's Cohutta Wilderness—significant climbing with wilderness scenery. The organization is solid, and the Georgia reputation is decent. However, it's a regional field, keeping it in Tier 3. Chatsworth provides moderate access. ### Finnish Gravel
Tier 2 | Score: 61/100 | 110 mi | 2,129 ft | Lahti, Paijanne Tavastia, Finland | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/finnish-gravel/

F1 glamour meets Nordic gravel in a UNESCO Geopark—premium experience, premium price tag, unique bucket-list appeal. Fast, flowing Finnish gravel through UNESCO Geopark terrain. Well-maintained gravel roads, lakeside routes, and Nordic ski paths create a high-speed experience rather than a technical challenge. The continuous undulations and steep kickers separate riders, but this is racing, not survival. Riders average 35 kph on the twisting ski path sections. Signature challenge: Not one killer climb, but the relentless accumulation of steep kickers and undulations that 'will separate' riders. Plus the bugs at every stop. FNLD GRVL is what happens when an F1 driver falls in love with gravel. It's not the most challenging course or the deepest pro field, but for a unique Nordic cultural experience wrapped in world-class event production, it delivers. The midnight sun, the UNESCO Geopark, the Finnish hospitality, and the SBT GRVL pedigree create something genuinely special. Yes if you want a unique Nordic cultural experience with world-class event production. Yes if you're an F1/Bottas fan. Skip if you're chasing big climbing or watching your budget. International travelers should weigh the high costs against the unique experience.

### Transcordilleras

Colombian Andes mountain gravel adventure. Colombian Andes mountain gravel. Signature challenge: Colombian Andes extreme mountainous terrain at altitude. Transcordilleras is a regional event in Colombian Andes—extreme mountainous terrain with massive elevation at high altitude. The Colombian Andes are stunning, but the difficult/expensive access, extreme altitude, and regional field keep it firmly in Tier 3. If you have a big budget and want an extreme Colombian experience—maybe. For most riders—there are better options. Non-negotiables: Altitude training; Mountain endurance; Budget planning

### WYO 131

It's a well-organized regional ultra event with good support, but the field is regional and it's remote central Wyoming. Gypsum is a small town near Vail with basic amenities. The course is challenging with 6000 feet of climbing at 7000-9000+ feet elevation. It's a well-organized regional event with a moderate field. ### Rasputitsa Spring Classic
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 60 mi | 5,000 ft | Burke, Vermont | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/rasputitsa-spring-classic/

Vermont spring mud season mountain gravel in Burke. Mountainous Vermont gravel with spring mud. Signature challenge: Vermont mountain climbing in spring mud season conditions. Rasputitsa Spring Classic is a Vermont spring gravel race—mountain terrain with spring mud season conditions. The unique spring mud challenge, significant climbing, and Vermont reputation elevate it above typical regional events, but the regional field keeps it in Tier 3. Burke provides moderate access. Well-organized for a spring mud event with 300-600 riders embracing the unique conditions. If you want a unique spring mud mountain challenge—yes, it's a legitimate test. If you want dry conditions or pro field depth—look elsewhere. Non-negotiables: Mud handling skills; Climbing endurance; Cold/wet weather preparation

### Sagan Fondo
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 75 mi | 7,000 ft | Truckee, California | June | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/sagan-fondo/

California Sierra Nevada/Tahoe gravel in Truckee. Tahoe/Sierra mountain gravel with significant climbing and altitude. Signature challenge: Tahoe/Sierra mountain climbing at 6000ft+ altitude. Sagan Fondo is a regional event in Truckee, California—Sierra Nevada/Tahoe mountain terrain with significant elevation and altitude. Named after Peter Sagan, it has good organization and the Tahoe/Sierra setting is spectacular. However, the regional field and California costs keep it in Tier 3. Truckee provides good access. If you're in California or visiting Tahoe—yes, it's a solid event with spectacular terrain. If you're from out of state—consider the costs and regional field. Non-negotiables: Mountain climbing endurance; Altitude preparation; Mountain pacing at altitude

### THE EQUALIZER - Missouri State Championship
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 100 mi | 5,000 ft | Marthasville, Missouri | July | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/the-equalizer/

Missouri's state championship is a heat-soaked slog through wine country rollers that separates the prepared from the hospitalized. Rolling Missouri River Valley terrain. 5,000 ft over 100 miles = constant ups and downs, no sustained climbs. Mix of smooth gravel county roads (85%) and paved connectors (15%). Terrain is rolling but not extreme. Heat is the defining challenge. Signature challenge: Peak temps (90-95°F) hit hardest mid-race. No shade. Heat index over 100°F. Where heat exhaustion happens. THE EQUALIZER is Missouri's state gravel championship—100 miles through wine country in late July heat. The course is rolling but not brutal (5,000 ft, constant ups and downs, no mountain climbs). The challenge is the weather: 90-95°F with 70-85% humidity means heat index over 100°F. This is a hydration and heat-management test more than a fitness test. The wine country setting is scenic, and Missouri gravel is smooth compared to mountain states. But the heat is relentless—15-20% DNF rates are heat-related. State championship brings some fast racers, but the field is mostly recreational (200-300 riders). St. Louis airport is 1 hour away, making travel easy. Marthasville is small, but nearby St. Charles has lodging. This is an emerging Midwest gravel scene event—not iconic, but solid. If you can handle 95°F with 80% humidity while grinding constant rollers for six hours, this is solid Midwest racing. The course won't break you—smooth gravel, manageable elevation—but July heat will cook anyone who shows up with two bottles and a prayer. Perfect for riders wanting state championship bragging rights without the technical demands of mountain races.

### Vuelta Altas Cumbres
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 100 mi | 12,000 ft | Córdoba, Argentina | March | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/vuelta-altas-cumbres/

Argentine Andes mountain gravel challenge. Argentine Andes mountain gravel. Signature challenge: Argentine Andes extreme mountainous terrain at altitude. Vuelta Altas Cumbres is a regional event in Argentine Andes—extreme mountainous terrain with massive elevation at high altitude. The Argentine Andes are stunning, but the difficult/expensive access, extreme altitude, and regional field keep it firmly in Tier 3. If you have a big budget and want an extreme Argentine experience—maybe. For most riders—there are better options. Non-negotiables: Altitude training; Mountain endurance; Budget planning

### Wasatch All-Road
Tier 2 | Score: 60/100 | 62 mi | 6,000 ft | Francis, Utah | August | gravel
Profile: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/wasatch-all-road/

Regional Utah mountain gravel with high altitude and significant climbing in Wasatch Range. Mountain gravel with significant climbing at high altitude. Signature challenge: 6000 feet of climbing at 6000-8000+ feet altitude in Wasatch Range. Wasatch All-Road offers a genuine mountain gravel challenge with high altitude and significant climbing in the beautiful Wasatch Range. Francis is a small town but proximity to Park City and Salt Lake City provides good access and amenities. The course is challenging with 6000 feet of climbing at 6000-8000+ feet elevation. It's a well-organized regional event with a moderate field. If you're in Utah or the Wasatch Front and want a mountain gravel challenge—yes, it's a well-organized event with genuine altitude and climbing. If you're traveling from far away—there are more prestigious mountain events elsewhere. Non-negotiables: Altitude preparation; Climbing strength; Thunderstorm awareness

| Dusty Gravel - Lacs de l'Eau d'Heure | 4 | 39 | 50 mi | 3,000 ft | Cerfontaine, Belgium | April | gravel |
| Dusty Gravel Series - Andenne | 4 | 39 | 50 mi | 2,500 ft | Andenne, Belgium | April | gravel |
| Haldon Heroic | 4 | 39 | 50 mi | 3,500 ft | Haldon Forest, Devon, UK | April | gravel |
| Nanaimo Unpaved | 4 | 39 | 50 mi | 3,500 ft | Nanaimo, British Columbia, Canada | June | gravel |
| OMG (Oxford MS Gravel) | 4 | 39 | 50 mi | 2,500 ft | Oxford, MS | January | gravel |
| Yorkshire Coast Dirt Dash | 4 | 39 | 45 mi | 3,000 ft | Whitby, UK | June | gravel |
| Rose City Rampage Gravel Race | 4 | 37 | 55 mi | 2,000 ft | Thomasville, GA | March | gravel |
| Rust Buster Gravel Fondo | 4 | 37 | 50 mi | 2,000 ft | Memphis, TN | April | gravel |
| Scratch Ankle Gravel | 4 | 36 | 45 mi | 1,500 ft | Milton, FL | March | gravel |

## Data Schema

Each race profile (JSON) contains:
- `race.name`, `race.slug`, `race.tagline`
- `race.vitals`: distance_mi, elevation_ft, location, date, terrain_types, field_size
- `race.gravel_god_rating`: overall_score, tier, 14 dimension scores, discipline
- `race.course_description`: character, suffering_zones, signature_challenge
- `race.terrain`: primary, surface, technical_rating, features
- `race.climate`: primary, description, challenges
- `race.logistics`: airport, lodging_strategy, official_site
- `race.history`: founded, origin_story, notable_moments
- `race.final_verdict`: one_liner, should_you_race, alternatives
- `race.non_negotiables`: training requirements with deadlines
- `race.citations`: source URLs
- `race.tire_recommendations`: primary tires, pressure tables

Access individual profiles at: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race/{slug}/
Machine-readable JSON index: https://gravelgodcycling.com/race-index.json
REST API: https://gravelgodcycling.com/api/v1/docs
